text: BORAS, or Bohras, a tribe scattered throughout Central and Western India, Jews in feature, habits, and character, but Mahommedans in faith, approaching nearest to the Shiahs, with a tendency towards Suffeeism. A peculiar dress worn during their religious services, and obedience to the spiritual authority of a multa, or high-priest, residing at Surat, are some of the peculiarities by which they are distinguished. Their name, in the 109. Gujjarati dialect, signifies 'merchants;' and they are in general very peaceable and orderly merchants and tradesmen.
BORASAMBAH, a pergunnah of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gundwana, 80 m. SW of Sumbulpur. It is dependent on Patna.
BORASTON, a chapelry in Burford p., in Salop. Pop. 199.
BORAU, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Aragon, 40 m. NNW of Huesca, and 9 m. NNW of Jaca.

BORBA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, in the comarca and 4 m. NW of Villa-Viçosa, and 18 m. WSW of Elvas. Pop. 3,500. It contains a castle, 2 churches, an hospital, and an alms-house. It was formerly fortified; but since its capture by the Spaniards, in 1662, has greatly declined in importance.

BORBA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the r. bank of the Madeira, 80 m. above the junction of that river with the Amazon. It is situated on an eminence, and is of considerable extent. The pop. consists of aborigines, Europeans, and Negroes. The surrounding district produces tobacco and cacao in great abundance.—Also a v. in the prov. of Minas- Geraes, in the district of Itaaibiru.
BORBI, a town of Turkey, in the prov. of Albania, I tent, being only 28 m. long, and 10 m. broad. The BOR in the sanjak and 32 m. ENE of Valona, near the r. bank of the Skrapari, 17 m. SE of Berat.
BORBINO. See ABADIA.
BORBOLEGAO, or BARBOLEGAO, a town of Portugal, on the SW confines of the prov. of Estremadura, 22 m. SSW of Alcacer do Sal, and 30 m. SE of Setubal.
BORBON, a town of Guayana, on the S bank of the Orinoco, 35 m. WSW of Angostura.
BORBON, or BOURBON, a fort of Bolivia, in the Llanos de Chaco, on the r. bank of the Paraguay, 360 m. SE of Santa Cruz de la Sierra.

BORBONA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra, in the district and 12 m. NE of Civita- Ducale, and cant. of Posta, on the slope of a mountain, on a tributary of the Velino. It has a parish church. Pop. 895.

BORBORE'MA, a great cordillera in Brazil, which commences at the Serra Araripe, or Dos Cayriris, between the parallels of 7° and 8° S, and runs SW, separating the prov. of Pernambuco from Piauhy, and the basin of the San Francisco on the SE from that of the Parnaiba on the NW.

BORCE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lower Pyrenees, cant. of Accous, in a valley, and on the Gave d'Aspe, 22 m. SW of Oleron. Pop. 785. In the vicinity is a warm mineral spring; and the surrounding mountains contain quarries of marble, and mines of copper and lead.
BORCIL. See Boreel.
BORCETTE. See Burtscheid.
BORCHENSDORF, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of E Prussia, 8 m. SE of Koenigsberg.

BORCHLOEN. See Looz. BORCHOLZ, Borgholz, or Borchett, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, in the circle and 10 m. NNE of Warburg, and 22 m. ESE of Paderborn. Pop. 1,169. It contains a synagogue, and a potash manufactory, and has a considerable trade in iron.
BORCHORST, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, in the gov. and 15 m. NW of Munster.
BORCHTCHA. See Bortesa.
BORD, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Boussac. Pop. 1,250.

BORDA (CAPE), the name given by the French to the NW point of Kangaroo island, off the S coast of Australia, in S lat. 35° 45½, E long. 136° 36' [Flinders], 136° 39½ [Freycinet]. The hills behind it are well wooded.
BORDEAN, a tything in Meon p., in Hants. Pop.

BORDEAUX, and more anciently BOURDEAULX, [Span. Burdeos, Ital. Bordo], one of the largest, handsomest, and most opulent cities of France, situated on the W or l. bank of the Garonne, in the dep. of Gironde, 55 m. SE from the mouth of the river; 55 m. S of Saintes; 90 m. S of La Rochelle, 280 WSW of Lyons, and 371 SSW of Paris; in 44° 50' 19" N lat., and 0° 34' 54" W long. Magnetic declination in 1830, 22° 15'. The etymology of the modern name admits of an easy explanation: for the site of B. is literally bord des eaux, or bordé d'eaux; but this, as Inglis observes, does not explain its ancient name Burdigala. Before the Revolution B. was the cap. of the Bordelais, and of the whole of Guienne; it is now the chief town in the dep. of the Gironde, and the head of an arrond. of 13 cantons, with an area of 419,115 hectares; and a pop. in 1836, of 247,748; in 1846, of 285,895. The cant. are Saint- Andre-de-Cubzac, Audenge, Belin, Blanquefort, B., Cadillac, Carbon-Blanc, Castelnau-de-Medoc, Creon, Labrède, Pessac, Podensac, and La Teste-de-Buch. The Bordelais, or district of B., is not of great exsoil, though sandy, is well-cultivated, and produces great quantities of the wine so well known by the name of vin de Bordeaux, or claret. These wines have long been famed for their purity of quality, limpid colour, and delicate bouquet and taste. Several of the white wines also of the B. are highly esteemed. The Bordelais now forms the greater part of the arrond. of B. General description.] The general outline of B. is that of a crescent, of which the chord, 23 m. in length, includes a portion of the bend which the river makes here, and of the suburb of La Bastide on its opposite bank. The greatest breadth of the city is 1 m. It occupies on the whole a much larger space than is absolutely necessary for its pop. Long rows of trees planted in the finest streets, magnificent public gardens and promenades, fill much of the space, and give to many of its quarters "a courtly rather than a commercial air." Yet the streets near the quays are always crowded, and full of bustle and traffic. B. has 19 gates, of which 12 are towards the river. It is not regularly fortified, but is surrounded by a wall and several old towers, and has two small forts called Ha, and St. Louis or St. Croix. It had formerly another and a much larger fort, called Château-Trompette, which was strengthened by Vauban in the time of Louis XIV., but was demolished in 1785, for a new quarter of the town. The suburbs of Chartrons and St. Sevrin are of considerable size. The town in the interior and older part is not remarkably handsome; the streets being for the most part crooked, narrow, and badly paved; but it has a number of handsome edifices, and the new parts of the city, such as the Quartier des Chartrons, and the Quartier du Chapeau Rouge, are very elegant. The place formed on the site of the Château-Trompette, at first named Place de Louis Seize, afterwards Place de Louis Philippe Premier, now doubtless rejoicing under another title, is the finest in B. The principal embellishments have been added since the American war. The town is built in general of a light kind of stone, very easily worked, which perhaps is one cause why the private hotels, and principal streets, are so magnificently decorated in the upper stories, while the street-floor is frequently occupied by petty shops. The most remarkable public buildings are the bourse or exchange, the custom-house, the ancient hotel, the palace founded by Bonaparte in 1810, a superb theatre erected in 1780, the old town-house, and the palace, first occupied by the dukes of Guienne, and afterwards by the parliament of B. The cathedral is a structure of great antiquity, and owes its origin to the English. It is 413 ft. in length, and has two light needle-like spires 150 ft. high. The church of St. Michael also was built by the English in the 12th cent. There are singular catacombs under this church; and on the summit of its isolated belfry, 178 ft. high, is a telegraph forming part of the line from Bayonne to Paris. Other churches are also interesting, but many of them were greatly injured during the Revolution. A very detailed account of the most interesting of them is given in Murray's Handbook. There are two Protestant churches.—The quays of B. are striking from their great breadth and length, the noble river which sweeps in a vast curve before them, and the line of well-built and regular houses which forms the back-ground. There is an air of mercantile bustle upon them: ships are unloading, cranes are working, and fine tawny specimens of the ox-tribe stand about in pairs, yoked by the horns, and ready to convey barrels and goods to the different warehouses. The new hospital is a noble institution with 710 beds. Bridge.] The bridge over the Garonne has 17 arches, and is 486 metres or 531 yards in length, and 50 ft. in breadth between the parapets. It was begun in 1811, and finished in 1821. On either side are commodious footways rising from the carriageway by steps of stone; and there is no perceptible change in its level as it passes over the river. The difficulties encountered in the construction of this noble bridge were great, for the general depth of the river beneath it is from 18 to 30 ft.; the current, with an ebbing tide, flows at the rate of 6 m. per hour; and the bed of the river is a loose sand. There are arched galleries between the crown of the arches and the level of the bridge, through which one is able to walk from one end to the other, and to view the quays from the centre of the bridge. There is also an aqueduct in the interior of the bridge by which the waters, which rise in the heights on the r. bank of the river, are conveyed into the city. The entire cost of this magnificent structure was £260,000. Manufactures and Commerce. The manufactures of B. are confined to a few branches, but are on a scale of considerable magnitude. There are several sugar-refineries, manufactories of tobacco, vinegar and liqueurs, shot-works, saltpetre-works, chemicalworks, and iron-founderies, and one or two atchers des subsistances, at which vast quantities of salted provisions, and sea-biscuits, are prepared for the navy. Besides these there are glass-works, and manufactories of earthenware, woollen stuffs, perfumery, and lac. The high price of labour and food, however, renders it improbable that B. will soon become an important seat of manufacturing industry. It was formerly renowned for its ship-builders, but no large vessels are now built here. The Garonne and Dordogne, and the canal of Languedoc, serve to convey to B. the produce of a very extensive tract of country, and carry back in return a quantity of merchandise both foreign and colonial. The maritime commerce of this city is, next to that of Marseilles and Havre, the most extensive in France, and owes its origin to the noble harbour here formed by the Garonne. The tide rises to the height of from 12 to 18 ft., and the quays and dockyards extend along the line of the river to a great distance, while the river itself is from 600 to 800 yards wide: so that large merchant vessels and even frigates can come up close to the town; and vessels, if not exceeding 600 tons, can come up at all states of the tide. On the 1st Jan., 1837, the vessels belonging to this port were 357, of a total tonnage of 66,355 tons. Of these 230 of a total tonnage of 57,500 tons, were employed in foreign-trade; and the rest in the coasting-trade. The total tonnage belonging to the port in 1840 was 409 vessels <math>=69,902</math> tons; of these 10 were steam-boats <math>=1,336</math> tons. In 1840 there cleared out from the port of B. 326 French vessels = 54,265 tons, with cargoes; 18 vessels = 2,040 tons, in ballast; 403 foreign vessels = 59,737 tons, with cargoes; and 69 vessels in ballast <math>= 16,498</math> tons. Total 816 vessels <math>= 132,540</math> tons. The total tonnage, French and foreign, which cleared at the ports of France in 1840 was 17,976 vessels = 1,847,395 tons. The coasting-trade of B. in <math>1835</math> employed <math>2,472</math> vessels <math>= 134,189</math> tons, manned by 13,949 hands. This was inferior to that of Marseilles and Havre. The former in the same year had a coasting-trade employing 4,127 vessels = 237,908 tons, and manned by 19,229 hands. The cabotage or coasting-trade of Havre, in the same year, was 2,521 vessels = 159,093 tons, and manned by 9,328 hands. In 1840 the coasting-trade of B. gave in entries 5,095 vessels = 169,056 tons, employing 18,273 hands. Wine is the staple export of B., and next to it are brandy, and fruits chiefly plums and almonds. The wines exported from B. are those of B., La Dordogne, Lot, Gers, and Lot-et-Garonne, and the brandies are those of B., Marmande, Cognac, Saintonge, and Languedoc. Previous to the Revolution, it was computed, that in time of peace 100,000 pipes of wine and 20,000 pipes of brandy were annually exported from B. by foreigners, in particular by the Dutch. In 1820, the export of wine was 61,110 hogsheads; in 1827, 54,492 hds.; at present it amounts to about 60,000 hds. The exports of vins ordinaires, in 1840, amounted to 48,024,009 litres: viz.— Of brandy, the exports in 1840 were 4,218,176 litres: viz.- To the Colonies, of the second section of Norway, English India, English India, Holland, Hanse-towns, . . . . . . Cattle, hides, clover-seed, oil, salted fish, cream-oftartar, verdigris, liqueurs particularly anisette, vinegar, raisins, chestnuts, walnuts, wood, hemp, madder, turpentine, cork, paper, honey, and hams, also form objects of exportation. The principal imports from England, are coal, tin, lead, copper, lamp black, machinery, and bricks; from Spain, iron, oil, liquorice, and saffron; from Holland, Denmark, and Sweden, staves, deals, timber for ship-building, hemp, pitch, iron, copper, and cheese. In 1840 the imports of sugar amounted to 17,054,544 kilog.; of rum, 220,175 litres; of coffee, 3,818,033 kil.; of cocoa, 1,713,131 kil.; tea, 429,368 kil.; rice, 1,872,627 kil.; pepper, 1,114,002 kil.; cloves, 139,404 kil.; cochineal, 13,763 kil.; indigo, 871,645 kil.; cotton-wool, 2,923,655 kil.; tobacco in leaf, 2,248,717 kil. The amount and value of bonded merchandise in B., on 31st Dec., 1840, was 19,202,856 kilog., valued at £1,444,537; in 1842 it was 24,099,104 kilog., valued at £1,087,266. The total value of bonded merchandise in the kingdom, on 31st Dec., 1840, was £6,634,993; and of this Marseilles held £2,133,464; and Havre £1,796,578. On 31st Dec., 1842, the total value of bonded merchandise in the kingdom was £8,132,380; and of this Havre held £3,066,841; and Marseilles, £2,498,008. B. is the chief port of France for colonial produce. The trade with the colonies employed 78 vessels <math>= 16,560</math> tons in 1840, and 87 vessels = 18,808 tons in 1842: viz. 1840. 1842. From Martinique, <math>23 \text{ vessels} = 5{,}115 \text{ tons} \quad 24 \text{ vessels} = 5{,}464</math> Guadaloupe, <math>=4,411</math> 19 <math>= 3.564</math> 23 Cayenne, <math>=1,498</math> <math>=1,876</math> 9 11 Senegal, <math>=1,209</math> <math>= 758</math> 7 Bourbon, 17 22 <math>=4,884</math> <math>=6,299</math> East Indies, <math>= 290</math> 1 The total entries of French vessels from the colonies at the different ports of France, in 1840, was 364 ships = 80,519 tons. The commodities sent to the colonies from B., exclusive of wine and brandy, are salted meat, manufactured goods and articles of clothing. The B. merchants take a part in the whale and cod fisheries, particularly the latter. The vessels engaged in the cod-fishery, which entered the port in 1840, were <math>60 = 7,840</math> tons; and the quantity of wet fish they imported was 131,493 cwt., and 10,381 cwt. dry, with 2,806 cwt. of oil. In 1842, 52 vessels = 6,777 tons, entered from the cod-fisheries. The intercourse with the United States is frequent, and is yearly on the increase. There are consuls-general I. here for Austria, Sardinia, Saxony, Sweden, and Hamburg; and consuls for Great Britain, Baden, Belgium, Brazil, Chile, Denmark, the Two Sicilies, Spain, the United States, Hanover, Mecklenburg- Schwerin, Mexico, Oldenburg, Holland, La Plata, Prussia, Russia, Saxe - Weimar, Switzerland, and Bremen. There is a joint-stock bank in B., with a cap. of 3,000,000 francs. The two yearly markets held at B. for a fortnight each, in March and October, are designed chiefly for the wine-trade.—There government such a tariff as would facilitate the introduction of British manufactures in exchange for the produce of Guienne; and from the British gov- Gironde to furnish an acceptable exchange for Brit- The duty levied on wine imported into Britain is 5s. 6d. per gall., and good wines can be had in B. for export at 3s. per gall.; nay, it is said, that, if free of duty, a good light wine could be landed at the London docks at something less to the importer than 4d. a bottle. Unfortunately, by our present tariff, whether wine sells at £400 or £40 per tun, it is subject to the same duty of £58. Now several of the lower classes of clarets are in fact esteemed, by competent judges, little inferior to those classed in the second ranks; the difference between the vins ordinaires, sold at from 300 to 400 francs per tun, and the fine wines of the Bordelais, arising from the small vine-growers seeking to obtain quantity at the expense of quality. But the high duties prevent the importation of the fourth and fifth class claret wines into Britain, which takes nearly half of the best sorts, but little of any other description. The whole quantity of B. wine of the very first growths does not exceed 1,200 or 1,600 hogsheads per annum. Its average value being from £30 to £40 per hhd., it can well afford to pay the duty of 5s. 6d. These wines for England are usually kept at B. for 3 years, and sometimes even 4, 5, or 6 years, before they are shipped, during all which time great attention is paid to fining them, drawing off the lees, and preventing fermentation, the cost of which is from 50 to 74 per cent. Climate, &c.] B. is in the neighbourhood of large marshes, and is most generally under the influence of W, SW, or NW winds, all of which, blowing over the ocean, or the Landes, come charged with moisture, hence it is not a healthy city. In summer the therm. ranges from 77° to 88°; in winter, it seldom descends below 41°. Kirwan estimated the mean temp. as derived from the observations of 5 successive years, viz. from 1777 to 1781, at 56° 8'. The mean oscillation of the barom., in 10 years at B., was 21.26 millim. The pop. of the com. and city was, in 1789, 82,602; in 1821, 89,202; in 1836, 98,705; in 1846, 120,203. B. is the see of an archbishop, who formerly took the title of primate of Aquitaine, and had under him 9 suffragans, and 450 parishes. The new ecclesiastical division gives him an immediate jurisdiction over the dep. of the Gironde, and authority over the diocesans of Poitiers, Agen, Perigueux, Lucon, La Rochelle, and Angouleme. B. is also the seat of a Calvinist consistory, and a consistorial synagogue. It is the seat of a cour royale, an academy founded in 1712, a royal college, a secondary school-of-medi- 3 G cine, a school of hydrography, a deaf and dumb institution; and possesses a public library of 115,000 vols., founded in 1738 by Lebel, a cabinet of natural history, a gallery of paintings, a botanical garden, and an observatory.-The communal revenue of B., in 1833, was 2,835,300 francs, of which 1,216,800 were raised by octroi. The disbursements of the communal administration amounted to 2,855,145 francs. Among the eminent characters to whom this city has given birth, may be mentioned the poet Ausonius, Berquin, Francis and Roger Ducos, Gensonné, Du Haillan, Boyer-Fonfrède, Charles Vernet, the two Lafiteau, and the ministers Martignac and Peyronnet. Montaigne and Montesquieu were born in the neighbourhood; and the former lies buried in the church of St. Bernard. History.] B. was known to the Romans by the name of Burdigala. Strabo mentions it under this name as the chief-trading place of the Bituriges. The Romans made it the cap. of Aquitania Secunda. In the 5th cent, it was in the possession of the Goths; in 509 it was taken by Clovis; in 729 it was pillaged by the Saracens; and it was repeatedly sacked and burned by the Normans. It was afterwards governed by the dukes of Guienne. By the marriage of Eleanora, daughter of William X., last duke of Guienne, to Louis of France, it was united, with its prov., to the French crown; but this lady being divorced by her husband in 1152, and afterwards marrying Henry, count of Anjou, who succeeded to the throne of England, B. became subject to the sovereigns of that country, by whom it was greatly improved. To this city Edward the Black Prince conducted his captive John II., king of France, after the battle of Poitiers. Edward made it the seat of his court for 11 years, and was one of the earliest authors of its prosperity. It was not re-annexed to France till the time of Charles in 1452. In 1548, a violent insurrection was occasioned here by the imposition of the gabelle, an oppressive salt-tax; among the excesses committed was the murder of De Moneins the governor. For this the town was severely punished by the constable Montmorency, who imposed a fine upon it of 200,000 livres, and executed its magistrates. During the progress of the Revolution, B. having attached itself to the party of the Girondists, the leaders of which were its own representatives, became the declared enemy of the Convention, and received a treatment similar to that experienced by Lyons and Marseilles. The most opulent and respectable inhabitants were either banished or guillotined, and their property confiscated. After the re-establishment of order under Bonaparte, the city was beginning to recover from its sufferings, when the war of 1803 broke out with England, and dissipated for a time all the fair prospects of its mercantile body. On the 12th March 1814, B. was the first place which declared for the Bourbons; and it was the last to submit to the restored authority of Napoleon in 1815. Authorities.] Bernadau, Histoire de B. 1838-40. 8vo.—Dictionnaire de la Conv. 1833.—Inglis Switz. and the South of France. - Bowring's 2d Report on the Commercial relations between France and G. Britain. 1835.—Macgregor's Commercial Tariff.—Parl. Papers.
BORDEAUX, a township in Abbeville district, in the state of S Carolina, 105 m. N by W of Columbia.

BORDELAIS, or Guienne-Propre, a district of France, in the ancient prov. of Guienne. It comprised an area of 854,649 hect., of which 754,149 hect. now belongs to the dep. of Gironde, and 100,500 hect. to that of Landes. Besides the cap., Bordeaux, above described, it contained the towns of Libourne, Bourg, Fronsac, Blaye, Coutras, and Lesparre.
BORDEN, a parish in Kent, 1 m. W of Sittingbourne. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 860.

BORDENTOWN, a town of Burlington co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 7 m. SE of Trenton. It is situated on the E bank of the Delaware, at an alt. of 65 ft. above the level of the river. The Camden and Amboy railroad passes beneath it, and the outlet of the Delaware and Raritan canal is in the immediate vicinity. This town, which is extremely neat and salubrious, is a favourite resort of the inhabitants of Philadelphia. France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées, arrond. of Bagnères - en - Bigorre. — The cant., comprising 18 com., had a pop. of 3,260 in 1831.—The com., 18 m. SE of Bagnères, on the Neste river, at an alt. of 884 metres, or 2,900 ft., has a pop. of 495.—Also a com- BOR mune, in the same dep., in the cant. of Tarbes. Pop. 1,773.
BORDES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariège, cant. of Castillon. Pop. 1,028.
BORDES (LES), a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Ariège, cant. of Le Maz d'Azil. Pop. 1,299.

BORDESHOLM, or BARDESHOLM, a town of Denmark, the cap. of a bailiwick of the same name, in the duchy of Holstein, on a small lake of the same name, 10 m. SSW of Kiel, near the line of railway from Kiel to Altona.—The bailiwick has an area of about 65 sq. m., and a pop. in 1846 of 8,820, composing 1,750 families.

BORDESLEY-CUM-DERITEND, a hamlet and chapelry in the p. of Aston, in Warwickshire, now forming part of the parl. borough of Birmingham. Pop. in 1841, 18,019.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Tardebigg, in Worcestershire, 3 m. SW of Broomsgrove.
BORDIANSK. See BERDIANSK.
BORDIGHIERA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 9 m. SW of San Remo, on the gulf of Genoa. Olive-oil is largely made here.
BORDOE, an island of Denmark, one of the Faroe islands, in the Norderöe-Syssel group. Pop. 300. It is intersected by the parallel of 62° 15' N.
BORDS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Saint-Savinien. Pop. 1,083.
BORDWELL, a parish in Queen's co., 3 m. NE of Rathdowney. Area 2,803 acres. Pop. 957.
BORDZICHOW, a village of Prussia, in the gov. of Dantzic, circle of Stargard. Pop. 224.
BOREA, a river of W Africa, which falls into the bight of Pannavia, 16 m. SSE from the mouth of the Malimba.

BOREE, a town in the prov. of Seistan, in Afghanistan, on the road leading from Dera Ghazi Khan, across the Suliman range, to the Kakur territory. It is situated in a fertile agricultural district, which has been compared, both in extent and fertility, to the plain of Peshawur. A considerable stream runs through B. to the SW.
BORE'E, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardêche, cant. of Saint-Martin-de-Valmas. Pop. 1,848.
BOREEL'S ISLES, several islets about 1 m. S of Tasman's head, on the SE coast of Van Diemen's land. The two easternmost are called THE FRIARS.
BOREGAUM, a town of Hindostan, in Candeish, 1 m. S of the Sukta river, 11 m. N by E of Asirgur. Pop. 500.
BOREK, a town of Prussia, in the prov. and 38 m. SSE of Posen. Pop. in 1837, 1,728, of whom 532 were Jews.
BOREKAM, a parish in Essex, 31 m. NE of Chelmsford. Area 3,820 acres. Pop. 1,054.
BORELAND, a village in the p. of Dysart, in Fifeshire. Pop. 193.
BORERAY, a small island in the Hebrides, 21 m. N of North Uist. It is 1½ m. in length, and a ½ m. in breadth. Pop. 181.
BORESFORD, a township in the p. of Brampton- Bryan, Herefordshire, 4 m. E by S of Knighton. Pop. 102.
BORGA, or Borgo, a town of Russia, in Finland, in the district of Nyland, 28 m. ENE of Helsingfors, on the Borgo-ä, near its embouchure in the gulf of
BORDE'RES, a canton, commune, and v. of Finland. Pop. 4,000. It is the see of a Lutheran bishop; and possesses a gymnasium, and a public library of 4,000 vols.
BORGENOE, or BERGENOE, an island of Norway, one of the Vigten group, 5 m. NW of the Outer Vigten, in N lat. 65°.
BORGENTREICH, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the gov. of Minden, 7 m. NE of Warburg, on the Bever river. Pop. 2,150. Potash is manufactured here.
BORGER, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Drenthe, 10 m. SE of Assen, near the l. bank of a head-stream of the Hunse.
BORGHAI. See BORKAI.

BORGHETTO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, in the deleg. and 15 m. SW of Verona, on the r. bank of the Mincio. Pop. 2,500. It has some slight fortifications. A sanguinary action was fought in the neighbourhood of B. between the French and Austrians, in 1796, in which the latter had the advantage.—Also a town of Sicily, in the district of Palermo.
BORGHETTO-DI-VARA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 14 m. NW of Spezzia, on the r. bank 500. of the Vara, opposite Brugnato. Pop. 354.
BORGHOLM, a town of Sweden, on the W coast of the island of Aland, of which it is the cap.
BORGHOLZ, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the gov. of Minden, on the Bever. Pop. 1,400.
BORGHOLZHAUSEN, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the gov. and 32 m. SW of Minden. Pop. in 1837, 1,270. Linens are made here.
BORGIA, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra 2da, 7 m. SW of Catanzaro. Pop. 3,497. Wine and silk are extensively produced in the environs. This town was nearly destroyed by an earthquake in 1783.

BORGNE, an extensive but shallow gulf on the coast of Louisiana, U. S., communicating with the gulf of Mexico by a channel between San Miguel kays and Cat island. Lake Pontchartrain communicates with it on the W side by two passages. New Orleans is within 11 m. of its W side. When the British attacked New Orleans in January 1814, General Packenham transported his artillery from the B. to the Mississippi by digging a canal, or enlarging perhaps an old water-course leading from a creek on the W side of this gulf into that river.
BORGO, a canton and commune of France, in the island of Corsica, arrond. of Bastia. The cant., comprising 5 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 2,138.—The com. and v. is 16 m. S of Bastia. Pop. 589.
BORGO-A-MOZZANO, a village of Lucca, on the 1. bank of the Serchio, 15 m. N of Lucca. Pop. 741.

BORGO-BRUND, or NAGY-Borgo, a village of Transylvania, in the circle of Doboka, at the entry of a pass leading through the Carpathian chain, from Transylvania to Galicia. It is celebrated for its pottery, and manufacture of pipes.
BORGO-COLLEFEGATO, a town of Naples in Abruzzo Ultra 2da, in the district and 23 m. SE of Citta-Ducale. Pop. 778.
BORGO-DI-VAL-SUGANA, a town of Austria, in Tyrol, in the circle and 17 m. E of Trente. Pop. 2,500.

BORGO-FORTE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 9 m. SSW of Mantua, on the l. bank of the Po. It is defended by a strong castle. On the 25th October, 1796, the Austrian general, Wurmser, was repulsed near this town by the French.
BORGO-FRANCO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 4 m. NNW of Ivrea, near the the l. bank of the Doire. Pop. 1,800.
BORGO-LAVEZZANO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 10 m. SSE of Novara, on the Arbogna. Pop. 4,000. Silk is extensively cultivated in the environs.
BORGO-MANERO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 20 m. NNW of Novara, on the Gogna. Pop. 6,000. There is a communal college here. fiord. BOR
BORGO-MARO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 10 m. NW of Oneglia, on the Impero.
BORGO-MASINO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 15 m. SE of Ivrea.
BORGO-NUOVO, a town of Parma, in the district and 10 m. WSW of Placentia.
BORGO-SAN-DALMAZZO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 6 m. SW of Cuneo, near the Sture. Pop. 3,000. Copper and iron are wrought in the neighbourhood.

BORGO-SAN-DONNINO, a town of Parma, 18 m. WNW of Parma, on the Stirone. Pop. 2,000 in 1833. It is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of Bologna; and has a theological seminary, and a printing-press. A ducal palace exists here.
BORGO-SAN-PIETRO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra 2da, 12 m. SE of Citta-Ducale. Pop.
BORGO-SAN-SEPOLCRO. See Sepolcro.
BORGO-SESIA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. of Val-Sesia, on the l. bank of the Sesia, 6 m. S of Varallo. Pop. 3,500.
BORGO-TARO, a town of Parma, on the Taro river, 35 m. SW of Parma. Pop. in 1833, 1,000. The district of B. had a pop. of 44,457 in 1833.
BORGO-TICINO, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 18 m. N of Novara, near the r. bank of the Tessin. Pop. 2,000.
BORGO-VERCELLI, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. of Novara, 21 m. NE of Vercelli. Pop. 2,500.
BORGSIO, a village of Sweden, in the district of Medelpad, on the l. or N bank of the Ljungan, 62 m. WNW of Sundsvall.

BORGU, a state, or rather federal union of petty states, in Sudan. It lies between the parallels of 10° and 15° N, and between 19° 30′ and 25° 30′ E long. On the N, it has the Libyan desert and Tibbu country; on the E, Darfur; on the S, the Nigritian desert and Begharmi; and on the W, Begharmi and Kanem. The country generally is flat and arid; but in the rainy season is covered with water in some districts. The Misselad appears to enter B. on the SE, and to flow in a NNW direction till it attains a nearly central point in the country, when it bends WNW to Lake Fittre, on the W frontier of B. The exact position and extent of this lake is not ascertained, but Denham was informed that it was 4 days E of Lake Tchad. The soil of B. is well-cultivated, and there is a considerable extent of forest-district within its boundaries. Dhourra and rice are extensively grown, and cotton is cultivated. The inhabitants are principally Moslems; and they are the westernmost Africans who use the Nishki, or Asiatic hand, in writing Arabic. They call their country Mobba; while the Arabs call it Dar Seleikh, and the Darfurians, B., or DAR B. It appears to bear the name Wadai in some accounts. Burckhardt was informed that the states composing B. were Warah, Silah, Runga, Tama, Mojo, Abasa, Mankari, Gimmir, and Jer; and that the principal towns have the names of the several states of which they are the capitals. Two caravan-routes traverse this region; one the route of the Sudan and Cairo caravan, entering it on the NE, and passing through it, by Wara, towards Dar Kulla, in a SW direction; and the other entering it from Fezzan, or from the Tibbu country, near the centre of its N frontier, running SE to Wara, and then turning E and NE to Cobbe in Darfur.
BORGUE, a parish in Kirkcudbrightshire, on the coast, about 10 m. in length, and from 3 to 7 m. in breadth. Pop. 1,117.
BORGUND, a town of Norway, in the district of N Bergen, on the N bank of the Lerdal, 16 m. above the influx of that river into an arm of the Sogne-
BORI, a town of Yarriba, in Sudan, on the l. or N bank of a river called the Moussa, which flows SE to the Niger.
BORI. See Buri.

BORINAGE, a district of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, celebrated for the extent of its collieries. It comprises the communes of Jemappes, Quaregnon, Hornu, Wasmes, Paturages, Frameries, Cuesmes, &c., with a pop. of about 32,000.
BORINGEN, or Bohringen, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black-forest, E of Urach. Pop. 843.
BORIQUEEN. See BIEQUES.
BORISLAWITZ, a village of Prussia, in Silesia, in the circle and 38 m. S of Oppeln. Pop. 320.
BORISSOGLEBSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 90 m. SSE of Tambof, on the Vorona, near its embouchure in the Khoper. Pop. 2,500.
BORITSH, a town of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle and 12 m. SE of Pilsen.
BORIZOFF, or Boryssow, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Minsk, on the l. bank of the Beresina, 32 m. NE of Minsk. Pop. 2,700. See Beresina.

BORJA, a town of Spain, in Aragon, the cap. of a judicial partido of the same name, in the prov. and 36 m. WNW of Saragossa, and 10 m. ESE of Tarazona. Pop. 3,000. It occupies a fine situation near the base of the Sierra Moncayo; and contains 3 churches, several convents, and an hospital. The family of the notorious Pope Alexander VI. take their name from this town. The surrounding district is extremely fertile and populous.

BORJA (SAN), a missionary settlement of S America, in the intendancy and on the r. bank of the Orinoco, 12 m. S of the confluence of the Meta with that river, and 35 m. NNE of Atures.-Also a town of Ecuador, in the prov. of Assuay, on the l. bank of the Maranon, 8 m. E of Santiago.—Also a town of Bolivia, in the district of Santa Cruz de la Sierra, near the r. bank of the Maniqui, 90 m. WSW of the confluence of that river with the Mamore.
BORJA (SAN FRANCISCO-DE-), a town of La Plata, in the prov. of Rio Grande do Sul, on the l. bank of the Uruguay, 70 m. NNE of Borrore.
BORJAS-BLANCAS, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, on an affluent of the Naguera Pallaresa, 12 m. ESE of Lerida, and 40 m. NW of Terragona.
BORK (NORTH), a town of Denmark, in the amt and 16 m. S of Ringkioping, near the SE bank of the Ringkioping-fiord.

BORK (South), a town of Denmark, in the amt of Ringkioping, 2 m. SSW of N Bork. BORKAI, BOR CHAYA, or BORGUI (BAY OF), an extensive and deep gulf of the Arctic ocean, on the Siberian coast, between the meridians of 129° and 133° E. Its NE extremity is formed by Cape Borkai, in N lat. 71° 50', whence the coast-line runs in a SSW direction to the parallel of 70° 44'; it then runs NNW to the mouth of the Lena.

BORKEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, cap. of the circle of the same name, in the regency and 36 m. WSW of Munster, near the l. bank of the Ahe. Pop. in 1837, 2,819. It contains 2 convents, and has manufactories of linen and velvet. It belongs to the prince of Salm, and had formerly a collegiate chapter of the order of Malta.—The circle of B. comprises a superficies of 108 sq. m., and contains a pop. of 36,170. It produces considerable quantities of grain and lint, and has some iron-mines. -Also a town of Hesse-Cassel, in the prov. of Nieder-Hessen, 22 m. SSW of Cassel, near the Schwalm. Pop. 1,150. It contains a church, a synagogue, and an hospital, and has manufactories of linen. Fairs are held three times a-year. BOR and 48 m. NNW of Poltova, in the district and 10 m. WSW of Zenkov, on the Groun-Tachanskaia.
BORKOWICE, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomirz, 15 m. from Opoczow. Pop. 350.
BORKULO, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, on the l. bank of the Berkel, 14 m. E of Zutphen. Pop. 1,240. It is fortified, and contains a fine military haras.

BORKUM, an island of the German ocean, belonging to, and 22 m. from, the NW coast of Hanover, in the prov. of E Friesland, and bailiwick of Aurich, between the E and W channels of the Ems, in N lat. 53° 36' and E long. 6° 40'. Pop. 485. It is 12 m. in circumf., and is so low across the centre as to be divided at high tide into two parts. It has a lighthouse. Fishing and the rearing of cattle form the chief occupations of the inhabitants.
BORLAND PARK, a village in the p. of Auchterarder, in Perthshire. Pop. 151.
BORLEY, a parish in Essex, 2½ m. NW of Sudbury. Area 730 acres. Pop. 188.
BORMES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Collobrieres, 24 m. E of Toulon, in a rocky plain near the sea. Pop. 1,599.

BORMIDA, a river of Piedmont, formed by the junction of the E and W Bormida, which take their rise in the Apennines, intersect the prov. of Millesimo and Montenotte, and unite in the prov. and 5 m. W of Acqui. Thence bending NNE, it receives the Orba on the right, and falls into the Tanara 2 m. E of Alexandria, after a total course of 72 m.-Also a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 15 m. W of Savona, near the source of the E Bormida.

BORMIO, called by the Germans Worms, a town of Venetian Lombardy, the cap. of a district of the same name, situated at the influx of the Fredolfo into the Adda. Pop. 2,500. In the neighbourhood, at Molina, there are excellent warm saline baths. From the German name of this town, the high ridge of Alps in the vicinity is called the Wormserjock. The district of B. terminates at the narrow defile of La Terra, where the traveller leaves behind him the Freddo paese, or 'Cold region,' and descends with the Adda into the rich Val Teline.
BORN, a village of Limburg, in the arrond. and 7 m. NE of Maestricht. Pop. 1,278.

BORNA, a town of Saxony, in the circle of Leipzig, situated between two arms of the river Wiehra, on the road from Leipzig to Altenburg. It contains, with the hamlets of Alstadt B. and Wenigen B., 3,500 inhabitants, who manufacture plush, woollen stuffs, and earthenware. B. was nearly burned to the ground in 1750, but was rebuilt in a neater style than formerly.
BORNAND (GRAND and PETIT), two towns of Sardinia, in Savoy, in the prov. of Faucigni. The former, 12 m. E of Annecy, has a pop. of 2,500. The latter, 6 m. S of Bonneville, has a pop. of 2,000.
BORNE, a river of England, which runs into the Tame, about 1 m. SW of Tamworth.
BORNE, a parish and v. of Holland, in the prov. of Over-Yssel, 4 m. NE of Delden. Pop. 2,600.
BORNECKE, a parochial village of the Prussian states, in the principality of Halberstadt, with 200 houses.

BORNEO, a magnificent island, in the centre of the great Eastern or Asiatic archipelago, stretching from 4° 20' S lat. to 7° N lat.; and from 106° 40' to 116° 45' E long. The equator divides it into two parts of nearly equal superficies. On the NE it has the Mindoro or Sulu sea, stretching between it and the Philippine islands and Mindanao; on the E, the Celebes sea, and the strait of Macassar, which latter divides it from the island of Celebes; on the S, the

A line drawn from Point Sambar, its extreme SW point, to Point Inarutang on the NE, is about 720 geog. m. in length, and from Point Mura, on the W to Kanuingan point on the E, or nearly along the parallel of 1° N, 600 m. But these are the extreme dimensions of the island. Its superficial extent can of course be only approximately estimated, as nothing like a survey of the island has ever been executed. By Stein its area is estimated at 9,893 German or 210,107 English sq. m., by the Almanach de Gotha at only 9,223 German sq. m. The Penny Cyclopædia estimates it at 286,000 sq. m.; Macculloch, and the English edition of Malte Brun's and Balbi's Geography, state it at 260,000 sq. m.; and Hamilton's East India Gazetteer at 262,500 sq. m. M. C. Moreau estimates it at 225,000 sq. m.; and M. Melvill de Carnbee at 6,992 sq. myriametres. Perhaps we shall not greatly err if we suppose this island to present a superficies equal to that of Great Britain and France united. Its coast-line exceeds 3,000 m. in length; and is indented by numerous fine harbours, and the estuaries of several rivers navigable far into the interior. Name.] The native name of this island is Pulo K'lemantan, or Kalamantan. The name Borneo, or rather Brunai, strictly belongs only to one Malay state on the island. Magellan, who saw this island in 1521, called it Burne. The Portuguese, in 1530, bestowed upon it the name which it has generally borne with European geographers since that period. Physical features.] B. exhibits the usual insular structure of the islands in the Eastern seas,—a mass of lofty mountains in the centre, sloping gradually down to level and alluvial tracts along the shore, which are in many places low and marshy, and in others present small hills covered with wood. The N part of the island is mountainous. The principal mountain in this quarter, Kina Balu, is at least 13,000 ft. high. [Bethune.] Mr. Earl doubts the existence of the great central chain which, in many maps of B., is represented as crossing the island from NE to SW; but there are numerous isolated hills; and the NE portion of the island is mountainous in character. It appears to be well-watered. The larger rivers probably rise in a great marshy table-land in the interior. The principal rivers known to Europeans are the Borneo, and the Banjarmassin, on the S coast; the Passir, and the Coti, on the E coast; and the Pontiana, and the Sambus, on the W. Towards the N extremity of the island is the lake of Kinibalu, which is said to be 100 m. in circumf. In about 1° 5' N lat., and 114° 20' E long, is a lake called Danaō- Malayu, which is about 24 m. long, by 12 m. broad; but in the rainy season spreads over a much larger area. It was first visited by Europeans in 1823. It is well-stored with fish; and contains two islands; but its depth does not usually exceed 18 ft. Climate.] The climate of B. is nearly the same as that of Ceylon; and is regarded as mild and salubrious beyond that of several of the neighbouring islands. Lieut. Barton states that the climate in the N part is rendered exceedingly agreeable, and even eool, by refreshing breezes; and that the temp. is sometimes so low in this quarter as to make woollen elothing desirable. It cannot, however, be salubrious in the thickly wooded and marshy districts which appear to form so large a portion of B. At Sakadana, the therm. is seldom under 82°, or above 94°; and the average summer-heat of the island is estimated at 84°. At Simpang, in the rainy season, the therm.

BORNEO. at 6 A. M. usually indicates 72° or 73°; at noon 83° or 84°; at 2 P. M. 86° or 87°; at 6 P. M. 76° or 77°. On the W coast, the wet season takes place during the SE monsoon, or from April to Sept.; on the N shores it occurs with the NE monsoon from Sept. to April. Capt. Bethune says he is inclined to think there is no decidedly dry season in B. Volcanic eruptions are not unknown. A recent and terrible eruption of a volcano, called Kloet, which had been inactive for a great number of years, has spread desolation throughout the prov. of Passaronan, one of the most fertile and salubrious districts of B. Showers of burning cinders were thrown up from the crater, which fell within an hour afterwards on the surrounding country, burning to cinders the crops, carbonising the plantations, and rendering the luxuriant plains an arid desert. At the same time, torrents of incandescent lava rushed from the sides of the volcano, and inundated the delightful district of Kedirie, enveloping in its waves the farms and villages, as well as the cattle and men who were surprised and overtaken by it.- The prevalent diseases at Pontianak on the W coast are diarrhœa, dysentery, remittent and intermittent fevers, rheumatic and bilious complaints, and smallpox. The cholera morbus has also made dreadful ravages here. At Sarawak, Europeans suffer from ague; the natives, chiefly from diseases of the eye and skin. Soil and Productions.] With regard to the greater part of B. there have as yet been but few opportunities of ascertaining the qualities and productions of its soil. The soil along the W coast is generally a rich mould formed partly of alluvial partly of decayed vegetable matter, about 2 ft. deep, resting on a white clay, which in many places is strongly impregnated with sulphur. That of Sarawak in B. Proper, consists, according to Mr. Low, of a rich strong yellow loam, of from 10 to 20 ft. in thickness, which is covered again by an average of 6 to 12 inches of a black and very productive vegetable mould, formed by the decayed vegetation of the primeval forests. This soil is admirably adapted for the cultivation of the sugar-cane, which flourishes in it without the slightest care from the natives, to greater perfection than in Ceylon under the skilful nurture of Europeans. An experiment has been tried to grow nutmegs-the cultivation of which is so costly at Singapore and Penang-in the cocoa-nut and other cheap styles of plantation, and with, we are assured, the most complete success. Oranges, cinnamon, cloves, and many other valuable exotics are also found to flourish in the soil of Sarawak, a fact which proves that the finer kinds of spices might be cultivated here with a little attention. Among the more ordinary, but not less valuable, products of the island, are enumerated -rice, sago, coffee, cotton, pepper, the cocoa-nut, tobacco, gutta percha, and many others. The camphor of B. is excellent. It is obtained from the Dryobalanops camphora of Colebrooke, which is a different tree from the Laurus camphora, and found only in Sumatra and B. The interior of the island abounds with this tree; and it is said that the natives will readily barter a bamboo full of camphor for one full of salt; yet it is so highly valued in China that it there fetches the price of £4 per lb. The prov. of Paitan is covered with forests of the camphor-tree, and the produce of them is as clear and well crystallized as the best sugar-candy. The timber of B. is often very large, but not generally suited for shipbuilding. The teak is not found in B. The slopes of the mountains are frequently covered with Casuarinas and a different species of pines. Above these we find gigantic specimens of the Diospiros or ironwood, of which the natives form their prahoes or canoes. Dipterocarpus, Quereus, and Myrtaceae, are found still higher up; the elegant Nepenthes covers the rocky precipices. The Quercus, Schima, Cleyera, Boschia, Spathodea, and other giants of tropical vegetation, are found in the valleys, and along the banks of the large rivers. The celebrated Upas tree is also found upon this island. The poisonous influence exerted upon those who approach it has been greatly exaggerated; but Captain Belcher relates that one of his crew, a powerful man of good constitution, was so much stupified while extracting some of the poisonous sap, as to be compelled to quit his position on the tree. Of fruit trees several distinct species of the Musa Paradisaica, or banana, and at least two varieties of the Artocarpus incisa, or bread-fruit, are cultivated. The mangostin, the mango, the citron, the pine-apple, and the pomegranate, are all indigenous; but any attempts to introduce the fruits of temperate countries have not yet been successful. The Piper betel, or betel pepper, the chili or capsicum, the Metroxylon sago, or sago-palm, and the Cocos nucifera, may be added to the list of culinary plants indigenous to B. Rice is the grain chiefly cultivated; maize and pulse are also grown. Animals.] The humid dense forests and jungles of B. are inhabited by an endless variety of the ape and monkey tribe, amongst which the Simia satyrus, or ourang-outang, is the largest and most remarkable, having been found only here and in Sumatra. Mr. Brooke obtained from a native the mutilated hand of an ourang-outang of enormous size. "This hand," he says, "far exceeds in length, breadth, and power the hand of any man in the ship; and though smoked and shrunk, the circumference of the fingers is half as big again as an ordinary human finger. The natives of B. call the ourang-outang the mias, of which they say there are two distinct sorts; one called the mias rombi—similar to the specimen aboard and the two in the Zoological gardens—and the mias pappan, a creature far larger, and more difficult to procure. To the latter kind the hand belongs. The mias pappan is represented to be as tall or taller than a man, and possessing vast strength: the face is fuller and larger than that of the mias rombi, and the hair reddish, but sometimes approaching to black. The mias rombi never exceeds four or four and a half feet; his face, unlike the pappan, is long, and his hair redder. I must own myself inclined to this opinion from various reasons:—1st. The natives appear so well agreed on the point, and so well acquainted with the distinction and the different names, that it is impossible to suppose it a fabrication for our peculiar use. Of the many whom I asked respecting them, at different times and in different places, most of them of their own accord mentioned the difference between the mias pappan and the mias rombi. The animal when brought aboard was stated to be the mias rombi, or small sort. In short, the natives, whether right or wrong, make the distinction. 2d. The immense size of the hand in my possession, the height of the animal killed on the coast of Sumatra, and the skull in the Paris museum, can scarcely be referred to an animal such as we know at home: though by specious analogical reasoning, the great disparity of the skulls has been pronounced the result merely of age." [Journal.] The S. nasicus and S. crystatus are also found here; and Muller's researches have added the Semnopithecus rubicundus, frontalus, and chrysomelas, to B. zoology. The Hylovatas concolor is the only species of gibbon that has yet been seen in B. The tiger of B. is the Felis macrocelis, an animal inferior in size and strength to the Felis pardus, which has not however been seen in B. The Ursus Malayanus exists in B.; and several species of Sciurus. It is generally supposed that neither the elephant nor the rhinoceros exist in B., although the former is found in Sumatra, and a species of the latter both in Sumatra and in Java. Several species of the Sus genus belong to B., and the tapir is of frequent occurrence. The Bos Sondaicus, called banteng by the Malays, occurs in large troops. The Napu and the Cervus russa are likewise numerous.— The ornithology of B. comprises several species of Calao, Breves, Couroucous, and king-fishers.—The Crocodilus biporcatus is found in B., as in all the Sunda islands; and a new species of this animal, betwixt the gavial of the Ganges and the crocodile properly so called, found in B., has received the name of the C. Schlegelii. The rivers abound in fish, and the seacoasts in turtles, oysters, and several varieties of shellfish. The sea-turtle, yielding the beautiful tortoiseshell, swarms along the E coast, whence the Chinese obtain about 30,000 lbs. weight annually. The pearlfisheries of B. might be made very productive. The sea washing the Sulu archipelago, or the long range of islands extending in a NE direction from the promontory of Unsang to the island of Bassilan, flows, it is reported, over one continuous bed of pearl-oysters. At present the Chinese obtain pearls to the value of about £10,000, and about 250,000 lbs. weight of mother-of-pearl, annually from these fisheries. Among the peculiar animal productions of B. are batio galiga, or bezoar stones, which are collected by the Dyaks from the muscular parts of animals, particularly the porcupine, and various species of Simia. Minerals.] The mineral productions of B. are of the richest kind. Coal abounds in Labuan, and on the NW coast of B. A coal-formation is found in Pulo Cheremin, an island at the embouchure of the Borneo river, extending along its coast, and running out towards the sea, by which it is covered at high water, and also taking a direction inland; but to what depth or extent has not as yet been ascertained. The coast of this island in fact presents a naked surface of coal, and it is probably from the glistening appearance which it has when the tide is out, that the island has obtained its name of Pulo Cheremin, or 'Mirror island.' Some of the most intelligent natives, by way of describing the quantity, had stated that there were mountains of coal, and that hundreds of ships might be laden with it. This intelligence is conceived to be of great interest, and, viewed in connexion with the future establishment of a steam-communication to China, of the highest importance. Specimens of the coal have been forwarded to Calcutta; and the subject would, it was thought, obtain the attentive consideration of the Supreme government, and lead not only to further inquiry and research, but to an arrangement with the B. chiefs to form depots of coal on the coasts, which for steamcommunication may be said to be on the high road to China. Antimony is found in large quantities. Iron is met with in all quarters, and is imported at Pontianak, both from Matan and from Banjarmassin. Tin, nickel, and quicksilver, are also found. The tin is of the kind called stream-tin-so abundant in Malacca and the Malayan peninsula; the nickel is found combined with iron and cobalt, and has not yet been worked. Mercury is known to exist, but hitherto it has not been obtained in sufficient quantity for the purposes of export. But as yet the most useful and profitable mineral obtained in Sarawak is antimony ore—being of a richer and more valuable kind than that obtained in Europe. It was the discovery by the natives of the value of this ore which produced that insurrection of the Dyaks which resulted in the elevation of Mr. Brooke to the rajahship, and laid the foundation of the permanent influence of England on the island. Soon after the establishment of the settlement of Singapore, the rajahs of Borneo, finding that the antimony of Sarawak commanded a ready sale there, forced the unfortunate Dyaks to work the mines, and exercised their cupidity and power with such wanton cruelty that the rebellion ensued, the putting an end to which was the price of the cession of the territory of Sarawak to Mr. Brooke.-Gold abounds. The principal gold-mines are in the vicinity of Sambas, on the W coast. An extensive tract here, of primitive formation, abounds in veins of gold; and about 30,000 Chinese, it is said, are here employed in obtaining the precious metal by washing in the alluvial districts, or extracting it from the rocky beds by mining. In Sarawak, Sambas, Sangow, and Banjar, it appears most to abound. In Sarawak it is found in all parts of the country on the r. hand or W part of the river, beyond the influence of the tides; it is found also in the S branch, but in less considerable quantities. In the crevices of the limestone above mentioned it is worked by Malays. "Last year," says Marryatt, "I accompanied Mr. Brooke on a visit to the rocks. The place they were then working was about 4 m. distant from the river, and about that distance from Seniawan and Thudong. This place was called Battu Kaladi, and was a limestone hill about 200 ft. in height, the surface of which was worn, -like all the limestone rocks of the country,-apparently by water, into ridges so sharp that it would have been exceedingly dangerous to have fallen upon them. Amongst these ridges were holes, exceedingly small, which penetrated into the heart of the mountain, some of them being 40 or more feet in depth. The only difficulty appeared to be in the labour of making the aperture sufficiently large to admit the miner; but this accomplished, on his descent he found the bottom, -which invariably opened to a cave,—covered with earth of a loamy nature. This, on being brought to the surface in baskets, was washed, and, it was stated, produced a bengkal of gold—about 13 oz. from each bushel of earth; from 6 to 10 or 12 bushels being found in each cave, according to its size. The Chinese, who are not permitted by the Malays to work in the rock, were quietly trenching the earth at the foot of the hill, which they had long worked for the same purpose, and with more certainty of profit, as it is not always that the caves, after the labour expended in getting into them, are found to produce the coveted metal. How the gold should be discovered in these fissures at all is very remarkable, and perhaps may afford a curious fact for the study of geologists and mineralogists. It cannot have descended from any place higher, as the caves are found in the highest as well as on the lowest parts of the surface of the flat-topped hill; nor, after repeated examinations of the limestone, is the slightest trace of the metal discoverable in it; the surface of the rock is but scantily furnished with earth, and that is of a vegetable nature. It is true that the whole of the soil of the surrounding district is alluvial and strongly impregnated with gold, but not to nearly so great an extent as that found in the fissures above described; hence the soil in these differs in the relative quantities it contains." The ore is of a pale colour, in consequence of containing from 10 to 15 per cent. of silver; but it contains little other admixture. It is impossible to form an exact estimate of the quantity of gold raised throughout B. Previous to 1818, when the Dutch seized this coast, upwards of 32,000 Chinese were employed in the gold-mines at Mantradu, and the western parts of B. Valuing the yearly produce extracted at 72 dollars each man, the sum total would amount to 2,224,000 dollars annually, or £556,000. But taking the medium quantity at 117 dollars per man, the sum total would be 3,744,000 dollars, or £936,000. In 1812, it was estimated that the annual amount of these mines amounted to 4,744,000 dollars, or £1,186,000, valuing the dollars at 5s. each. "As the mines," says Sir Stamford Raffles, "are worked with so little expense of machinery, and as the property of the soil belongs to the first occupant, almost every Chinese would become a proprietor, but for the mode in which their services are, in the first instance, secured by the council of proprietors or kongsis. A parcel of half-starved Chinese, enchanted with the prospects of wealth on the golden shores of B., readily find a passage in the annual junks that sail from the mother country at 10 dollars a-head. On their arrival, being unable to pay the passagemoney, and the tax of a dollar per head, established by the native authority, while their immediate wants for food and habitation are imperious, the proprietors of the mines find it easy to engage their services for three or four years. In some other cases agents are employed to obtain men from China, on stipulated agreements to work for a number of years; the usual rate of payment to the miners so engaged is not considered to average less than 5 Spanish dollars a-month. No sooner, however, are these engagements concluded with their masters, than a number of them club together with the funds they have been able to save, and commence a new mine upon their joint account, in a few years acquiring a competency to return to their native country." The sum annually remitted by these industrious emigrants to China was £500,000; and about one-half of that sum was remitted to Bengal and the western part of India, and the surplus went to Java. Silver is nowhere found in B., except as an alloy of gold. Copper ore has been discovered in vast deposits at Mandore and at Mampava, both on the W coast.—The diamond-mines are confined to the W and S coasts, being principally situated in the territories of Pontianak and Banjarmassin. The resident Bugis are the great dealers in diamonds. The rajah of Matan is in possession of one of the largest diamonds known to exist. It was obtained about 100 years ago from the mine at Landak, 22 leagues from Pontianak, and weighs 367 carats gross. Its estimated value is £269,377. The diamonds of B. have generally a white or a yellow hue; none of them exhibit the inky tinge so highly valued in those of Golconda. The small stones are sold at Pontianak, and the large ones are disposed of at Batavia, and the profits equally divided betwixt the Dutch gov. and the Panambahan. The beautiful crystals called water-diamonds are found in various parts of the island; but these can only through fraud or ignorance be confounded with the genuine carbon diamond. Population.] The pop. of B. has been estimated—we should suppose above the truth—at 3,000,000. M. C. Moreau, upwards of 20 years ago, estimated it at 3,500,000; but on altogether insufficient data. The interior is entirely occupied by a native race, bearing the general name of Dayaks or Dyaks,—pronounced dyah—but variously named according to the parts of the island which they inhabit, and nearly similar in character to those who occupy the interior of Sumatra. Some of these tribes cultivate the ground, —others display considerable industry in fishing,—and a few employ themselves in collecting gold; but their institutions in general indicate the rudest state of human society. In personal appearance they are decidedly superior to the Malays, and their women are rather good-looking. Their colour is dark, and resembles that of the Malays, but they never intermarry with the latter. The chastity of the females is remarkable. They are decidedly good-looking, much fairer than the men, well shaped, and remarkable for enbonpoint. On them falls all the labour and drudgery of the house, the grinding of rice, the fetching of burdens, the carrying of water, fishing, and field-work:-yet they are cheerful, good humoured, franker than the men, and by no means shy beneath the gaze of strangers. All parties seem to be agreed in regarding the Dyaks as the aborigines of this island. They are altogether a peculiar race of men, distinct in manners and appearance from all the other numerous tribes of the archipelago. They are divided into numerous tribes; and appear to become more savage as they approach the central parts of the island. Their language is a branch of what Marsden and Crawford call the Polynesian: they have indeed different dialects, but understand each other. They do not practise tattooing; nor do they all use the sumpitan or blow-pipe as a weapon. The dress of the men consists of a narrow cloth which, wrapped round the loins and passing between the legs, hangs down before and behind; a shorter cloth is wrapped round the head; and a sword, knife, and betel-box, complete a man's accourrements. The women wear a cloth folded round the waist, and descending to the knees. Both men and women ornament their legs and arms with rings of brass, wire, shells, &c. They are fond of dancing; and a dance always forms part of a ceremonial reception. It has been reported that the Dyaks devour the flesh of their enemies,—an assertion probably in this, as in many instances, without foundation. All accounts agree, however, as to another truly savage custom observed amongst them, by which every man is debarred from the privilege of matrimony until he has, with his own hand, cut off the head of an enemy. Those who are desirous of entering into this state, form themselves into what Dr. Leyden calls "headhunting expeditions," and make an inroad into the territories of a neighbouring tribe; if their strength appears sufficient, they endeavour to effect their objects by force; if otherwise, they conceal themselves behind thickets till an unfortunate individual passes, whom they can make their prey. When a married woman dies, her husband is not allowed to take a second wife until he has slain an enemy in battle, and offered his head in sacrifice to the manes of his deceased wife. Some are also said to immolate human victims on the altars of their divinities. Captain Keppel visited the location of one of the Dyak tribes, which is thus described: - "Tungong stands on the left hand close to the margin of the stream [the Batang-Lupar], and is enclosed by a slight stockade. Within this defence there is one enormous house for the whole pop., and three or four small huts. The exterior of the defence between it and the river is occupied by sheds for prahus; and at each extremity are one or two houses belonging to Malay residents. The common habitation, as rude as it is enormous, measures 594 ft. in length, and the front room, or street, is the entire length of the building, and 21 ft. broad. The back part is divided by mat-partitions into the private apartments of the various families, and of these there are 45 separate doors leading from the public apartment. The widowers and young unmarried men occupy the public room, as only those with wives are entitled to the advantage of separate rooms. This edifice is raised 12 ft. from the ground; and the means of ascent is by the trunk of a tree with notches cut in it—a most difficult, steep, and awkward ladder. In front is a terrace 50 ft. broad, running partially along the front of the building, formed, like the floors, of split bamboo. This platform, as well as the front room, besides the regular inhabitants, is the resort of pigs, dogs, birds, monkeys, and fowls, and presents a glorious scene of confusion and bustle. Here the ordinary occupations of domestic labour are carried on-badi ground, mats made, &c. &c. There were 200 men, women, and children counted in the room and in front whilst we were there in the middle of the day; and allowing for those abroad and those in their own rooms, the whole community cannot be reckoned at less than 400 souls. Overhead, about 7 ft. high, is a second crazy story, on which are stowed their stores of food and their implements of labour and war. Along the large room are hung many cots, 4 ft. long, formed of the hollow trunks of trees cut in half, which answer the purpose of seats by day and beds by night. The Sibnowan Dyaks are a wild-looking but apparently quiet and inoffensive race. The apartment of their chief, by name Sejugah, is situated nearly in the centre of the building, and is larger than any other. In front of it nice mats were spread on the occasion of our visit, whilst over our heads dangled about thirty ghastly skulls, according to the custom of these people. The chief was a man of middle age, with a mild and pleasing countenance and gentle manners. He had around him several sons and relations, and one or two of the leading men of his tribe; but the rest seemed by no means to be restrained by his presence, or to show him any particular marks of respect-certainly not the slightest of the servile obsequiousness observed by the Malays before their prince. Their dress consists of a single strip of cloth round the loins, with the ends hanging down before and behind; and a light turban, composed of the bark of trees, round the head, so arranged that the front is stuck up somewhat resembling a short plume of feathers. Their figures are almost universally well-made, and showing great activity without great muscular development; but their stature is diminutive." The religion of the Dyaks is little understood. Recent observers have fancied that they discerned some traces of Hindu origin in it. A mission under the auspices of Rajah Brooke of Sarawak, and the Episcopal church of England, is about to undertake the evangelization of the native tribes of B. in the neighbourhood of Sarawak. The promoters of this excellent work believe that there are peculiar elements of success both in the present social condition of the Dyaks and in the state of their religious belief. We are told that "of the religion of the Dyaks little is known, because probably there is little to be discovered among a people who have lived for centuries in a purely savage state, divided into numerous separate tribes, with a slender acquaintance with the operations of nature, and whose creed, like their other ideas, must be of an insignificant and feeble character, not extending probably beyond a very indistinct perception of a Supreme Power and some other existence after death. Still the absence of prejudices, like that of caste, so powerful an obstacle in India to the reception of Christianity, the absence of prescribed and hallowed rites which so often influence the passions under a more perplexed and deformed system of popular idolatry, as well as the absence of any distinct order set apart for the performance of the functions of religion, must be regarded as favourable circumstances, especially among a people who have considerable physical advantages, and whose character is said to be simple, truthful, and honest, contrasting in these respects most favourably with other uncivilized races. Persons best acquainted with these interesting barbarians describe them as similar in character and condition to the inhabitants of the Philippine islands, where, from similar advantageous circumstances, the Spanish missionaries found the work of conversion so speedy and universal." The Malay pop. of B. would bear, Mr. Low thinks, no unfavourable comparison with the lower classes of European nations. They are Moslems in religion, and of the Sunni sect. Their spiritual superiors exert great influence over them. "When addicted to piracy they are deceitful, revengeful, rapacious, and cruel; but it has been remarked by Mr. Brooke and others, that when peacefully employed, they are amiable, fond of children, courteous to strangers, and grateful for kindness. Their migratory and maritime habits, their intercourse with others, and especially their modern and more intimate acquaintance with European commerce and power, have rendered them less severe in their religious discipline, and less jealous of their theological creed than many other Mahomedan nations." The Chinese are Buddhists, and it appears that they evince great willingness to be instructed, and have no strong prejudices against Christianity. A recent writer in the Singapore Chronicle estimates the number of Chinese in B. at 125,000. They are employed in mining, agriculture, sugar-making, and fishing; and as artificers, and petty merchants.—Rienzi enumerates, in addition to these leading races of pop., Punams, Biajus, Tiduns, Kayans, Assums, and Marats. There are a few Arabs and their descendants. Territorial divisions.] According to an arrêté of the governor-general of the Dutch possessions in India, dated Feb. 28, 1846, the territorial divisions of B. are as follow: I. Western Coast, comprising the two residencies of Sambas and Pontianak. 1. Sambas comprises the coast-district from Tanjong-Datoe to the embouchure of the Soengi-Doeri; and the following islands: viz, Baroe, Lomboekoetan, Penatah, Kabong, Saloewar, Landias, Pika, Pontianak, Kambang, Toewah, and Gading. The interior district of Sambas is defined by a line drawn from the summit of Mount Pangi, along the highlands of Tanjong-Datoe, to Mount Bajang; and from Bajang, along Mounts Sebahoe, Pandan, Sebakal, Sempoeroe, and Pahoedjan, and the L bank of the Soengi Doeri, to the sea. 2. Pontianak comprises the coast from the Soengi Doeri, to Mount Penampoengang on the E coast, and thence to the sea; and the following islands, viz.: Setienjang, Damar, Penemboengon, Temadjoe, Datoe, Koembang, Malang-Merakit, Nanas, Antoe, Masatiga, Karimata, Togongperangin, Togongkrawang, Semoer, Oemah, Ajer, Oebang, the four Pappan isles, the three Maladang isles, Mentiegi, Lajak, Pandan, Lesing, Grisèk, Balangoer, Genting, Besi, the two Boerong isles, the two Auwer isles, Aro, Seroetoe, Bilian, Boesong, Goenong, Genteng, Sorong-gading, the two Boeloe isles, Boelat-kebajang, Kerra, Lintang, Bakau, Karemata-toewah, Boewan, Nibong, Sokot-oeloi, the four Melapis, Boeleh, Genting, Sirih, Panambangan, Troesan-hadji, the four Leman isles, Pelintoean, Salanama, Datoe, Djoanta, Katoeng, Penjam, Nibong, Lalang, Agoen-Pisang, Sambadin, Tjampedak, Tjibeh, Tjambedak-salat, Langgir, Pananggon, Tjoekoes, Toekangmang-koedan, Sawi, Djamboh, Koetjing, Nanas, Loekoetkera, Djeras, Langan, Bauwat, Gilang, Penamboen, Mangkot, and Batoetiti. From the frontiers of Sambas, and Mount Bajang, the boundary line of this residency is made to run along the water-shed dividing the rivers flowing towards Broeni from those which flow into the Kapoeas. It then proceeds in an E and NE direction to the high chain of mountains which forms the interior frontier of Berow, which it strikes on the parallel of 3° 20' N. From this point it runs along the Kenimting chain, and turning S, W, and WSW, to Mount Pabaringan-badakh, and thence to Batoehadji, runs in a direct line to the coast.—The districts of Pontianak, Mampauwa, Landakh, Koeboe, Simpang, Succadana, Matan, Tajan, Meliauw, Sangauw, Sekadauw, Sintang, Melawi, Sepapoe, Blitang, Silat, Salimbauw, Piassa, Djongkong, Boenot, Malor, Taman, Ketan, Poenam, and certain Dayak tribes of the interior, are represented to be dependent on the residency of Pontianak. II. Eastern and Southern coast, defined by a line running from the river Kotaringen, on the frontiers of Pontianak, W, NW, and N, to Kampong- Atas, on the frontiers of the kingdom of Boelongan; and comprising the following islands, viz., Damar, Datoe, Laut, Laut-ketjil, the Moresses, Dwaalder, Nangka, Meang, Mataka, Bilang-bilangan, Balikoekoep, Maniboera, Pandjang, Derawan, Roba, Samama, Taba, Kakabang, and Maratoewah. The kingdom of Banjarmassin is comprised within this division as a dependent state; the states of Berow, Pasir, Tanaboemboe, Mandawei, Sampit, Pemboeang, and Kotaringin; and the districts of Tarrah-laut, Doeson, Pekoempai, Poelve-Petak, Kahajan, and Kapoeas. III. The districts of B. not under Dutch influence, according to the arrêté of 1846, were,- 1. The states of the sultan of Broeni, or Borneo Proper, on the NW coast, from Tanjong-Datoe on the W, to the Kemanis on the E. 2. The states of the sultan of the Sulu isles, bounded on the W by the river Kemanis, and on the E by the river Atas, separating them from Berow. 3. All the islands lying off the N coast of B. The sultan of the Sulu islands claims the sovereignty of the NE coast of B: from the river Kemanis on the NW coast, as far as Cape Koniugan. The native tribes dwelling along the banks of the Kemanis are called Idaan by Dalrymple, which Leyden considers only another name for Dyak. On this coast we find the rivers of Pangalet, Pappal, and Mangeabung, all of which have a Malay pop. on their banks in the lower part of their course. On the river Tavaran, which, it is said, is navigable for boats as far as the great central lake of Kinibalu, various tribes of Dyaks, and a considerable number of Chinese, are settled. The river of Tamposak is also represented to flow from the great central lake to this part of the coast, on which we also find the rivers Lu and Tabalulan, the harbour of Abai, and the deep bay and harbour of Barkoku, which approaches, it is reported, to within 40 or 50 m. of Lake Kinibalu. The productions of this part of the coast are sago, rice, betel-nut, cocoa-nut oil, camphor, wax, pepper, cinnamon, rattans, clove-bark, and pearls. Its trade appears to be chiefly in the hands of the Chinese; but little is known of the coast in this quarter, as the inhabitants have all the piratical habits of the adjacent Sulu islanders; and after the English settlement on Balambangan, opposite Cape Sampanmangia, was abandoned, their piratical practices were without check, until English influence, from B. Proper, again restrained them. From Cape Koniugan to Cape Salatam, the SE coast is divided among a number of petty chiefs, over whom the Dutch affect to exercise authority. On the long peninsular projection of Unsang, the Spaniards at one time had a settlement, which they soon abandoned. On this coast we find the bays of Sugut and Lubuk, into the former of which a river, descending, it is reported, from Lake Kinibalu, discharges itself. E of Lubuk lies the island of Bahela-tulis, which forms the entrance of the bay of Sandakan, which is about 5 leagues deep, and has three good harbours. Between Sandakan and Tambisan, the NE point of the promontory of Unsang, are the mouths of thirty water-courses, all of which are said to be deltoid branches of the great river Kinabantangan, which also is traced to the great interior lake of Kinibalu. On the S coast of Unsang, are the rivers and bays of Sibait, Tunku, Malaburg, Babatu, and Saturung. From the large bay of Jiong, which contains several islands and shoals, to the river Tawaw, the coast is much broken by creeks, inlets, and the estuaries of rivers. The districts now described, forming the most eastern portion of B., produce gold, camphor, tortoise-shell, agar or eagle-wood, lacka, a species of red wood, and trepang. The coast of Tirun, lying to the S of the river Sibocu, is in general low, swampy, and overgrown with mangroves. Dalrymple says that its inhabitants speak a language equally unintelligible to the Sulus and the Idaan or Dyaks. A large portion of this district is covered with sago-trees. Its chief productions are gold, wax, honey, bezoar, mats, canes, rattans, and trepang. Many Anamese have settled on this coast. Its rivers are numerous, and some of them navigable.-The district of Balangan or Barunyan, lies between the Tirun country and the state of Barow or Berow, which, when Dalrymple wrote, was tributary, with the inferior state of Kuran, to the Sulu sultan .- S of Berow lies Talisyar; and to the S of the latter, Samauanti.—The town of Coti or Koté is situated on a river of the same name, about 50 m. from its embouchure, and considerably to the S of the frontiers of Samauanti. It is inhabited chiefly by Bugi traders, who annually visit Singapore in their prahus or trading-vessels. The banks of the Coti are said to be densely populated. The enterprising traveller Muller was murdered on the banks of this river in 1825.—To the S of Coti lies Aparkarang, a small Dyak state, on the coast of which is a dangerous bay, called Balikpapan.—The state of Passir lies between Aparkarang and Banjarmassin. Its cap. consisted of about 300 houses, chiefly inhabited by Bugis, situated 45 m. up a large river of the same name. These Bugis have since withdrawn to Pulo Laut, an island about 40 m. in length, by 20 m. in breadth, 180 m. S of the river Passir, and separated from the SE angle of B. by the straits of Pulo Laut. In 1772 the English attempted to form a settlement at the mouth of the Passir, but the scheme failed. On the E coast of B. there is a great mixture of the Bugis blood amongst the Malays; all the rajahs and principal men are Bugis, and more or less related to families in Celebes. The state of Banjarmassin, on the S coast of B., has already been noticed under its own head. It lies nearly opposite the island of Java, and had considerable commerce both with Java and Singapore, until of late years, when Dutch influence became predominant here.
