It is intersected from N to S by the Banjar, Bandjir, or Soengi Doeson river, the largest in B.; to the W of which occur in succession the Kahajan or Great Dyak, the Soengi Sibangau, the Mandawi, the Sampit, &c., all rivers of slow course, and from 3 to 6 fath. in depth for at least 50 m. from the coast. They rise probably in a range of mountains to the N, called by the Malays Gunong Malawi Pino. Colonel De Henrici estimated the pop. of Banjarmassin directly under the government of its own sultan, in 1836, at 60,000 souls; and the pop. of the Dutch territories in this state at 40,000. These estimates were exclusive of the independent Dyak tribes, who might perhaps amount to 500,000. The districts which have been longest and most directly under Dutch influence, stretch along the W coast of B. The principal are Simpang; Matan, now called Succadana, on both sides of a large river of that name; to the N of Succadana lies the great river Pontiana; to the N of which again is the district of Mampava or Mampauwa; and still further N, stretching to the W limits of Borneo Proper, the Sambas territory. All these districts or states are comprised in the Dutch territorial divisions of B., in the western residency. That gov. has acquired this portion of their colonial dominions by treaties with the native princes, of which the general principles are, that, in consideration of the ports being placed under the immediate control of the Dutch, and of the respective chiefs or sultans, not negotiating with any other European or American states, these princes shall receive a monthly salary from the Dutch gov. The principle of the treaties with the Malay and Dyak chiefs, in the interior, is that their territories shall be administered by the Dutch, their persons and states protected, and the revenues equally divided. The face of the country is generally low; and the great lake Danao Malayu, although 45 leagues from the nearest point of coast, is probably not more than 100 ft. above sea-level. The highest land is at the back or to the E of Sambas, and between that district and Mampawa. In some places appear detached, conical-shaped, and peaked hills, similar in appearance and structure to the islands along the coast; while rocks which appear to have been exposed to a surf are found in the interior; and the low land along the coast has advanced considerably even since the establishment of European ports. So that this part of the island appears to have been recently raised from the bottom of the sea. The entrances of all the rivers are obstructed by bars. On that of Pontianak there are 8 ft.; on Sambas 15 ft.; on Mompawa 6 ft., at high-water spring-tides; and the rise of the tide is from 6 to 8 ft. The pop. of the whole residency— which appears by the Dutch maps to embrace nearly one-third of the whole island—was estimated in 1825 at 590,100, exclusive of Europeans. Of this number 237,720 were dependent Dyaks; 134,946 Malays and Arabs; 80,000 independent Dyaks; 36,074 Chinese; and 11,360 Bugis. Pontiana, or Pontianak, the cap. of this residency, had a pop. in 1836 of 56,637 souls; of whom only 22 were Europeans; 319 were Arabs; 2,211 Bugis; 3,001 Malays; 13,391 Dyaks; and 17,693 Chinese. The exports from this residency are gold, diamonds, wax, rattans, dammer, oils, a little rice, and some coarse sugar. The imports are opium, piece-goods, iron, tobacco, salt, silk, pulse, oil, and a few Chinese goods. The remaining part of the NW coast of B., from Tanjong Data, falls to be described under the head of Borneo Proper. Between Sambas and Borneo, when Leyden wrote, were a number of small independent piratical states, governed by Malay chiefs, called pangerans. He enumerates Serawa, the now well-known Sarawak, Klakka, Mokka, Siribas, and Palo. History.] The Spanish navigator Magellan was the first European who visited B. He saw it in 1521. In 1527 it was visited by the Portuguese. In 1598, Olivier van Noort, a Dutchman, touched at it with four trading-vessels; and in 1609, the Dutch obtained permission to erect a factory on the coast of B. from the sultan of Sambas. In 1702, we find the English established on the S coast of B., at Banjarmassin. This state, it appears from an article in the Moniteur des Indes Orientales—a Dutch authority was founded by Ampudjatonaka, the son of a merchant of Coromandel, whose family continued to govern the soil for several generations, until 1664, the period when the Dutch first held commercial intercourse with it. The reigning prince was at that time called sultan Said. A treaty passed between him and the Dutch, to which the former, however, at the instigation of the Portuguese, then the rivals of the Dutch, did not keep faithful. These first relations were therefore of no importance. Said died, and left his son under the guardianship of an uncle, who on the death of his ward ascended the throne with the title of sultan Tahlil. This prince leagued himself with the English, who came for cargoes to Banjarmassin, and succeeded in expelling the Dutch from the island, where they had recently established themselves. The English then sought to establish a factory on the coast; but after a brief period it was plundered in 1707 by the natives, acting under the orders of the saltan, who did not long survive this cruel act. He was succeeded by his son, sultan Tahmid, under whose reign the Dutch East India company renewed their treaties of commerce with Banjarmassin. The company obtained the monopoly of the spice trade; and a short time later, by a new contract, the exclusive monopoly of pepper was given to them, as well as permission to erect a fort at Tabenio, and a factory in the island of Tatas. In return for these concessions, the company engaged to assist the sultan against his enemies. At the death of Tahmid, his son, Tamsid, mounted the throne. His reign was disturbed by a rebellion of the inferior chiefs. The sultan claimed the assistance of the company to quell the insurrection, and in 1756 a treaty was concluded, in which he was promised assistance both by land and sea. The rebellion was quelled, and as recompense for their services the company received under their sovereignty the provinces called Passir, Koetei, Berou, and Kotta Waringui. The sultan died, leaving two sons, the eldest of whom, Tahmid by name, succeeded him. Revolts having again taken place, were put down with the assistance of the Dutch, who again received grants of provinces in return. At last, in 1787, the company obtained of Pangeran Natta, who had usurped his brother's place, the sovereignty of the whole kingdom, he remaining nominally at its head, and holding it only as an hereditary fief. This prince left the succession to his son Tleman. Under the administration of governor-general Daendals, the establishment of Tatas was abandoned in 1809; but the real rights of the government were not thereby given up. Finally, when Helland recovered possession of her colonies, in 1816, B. became again in the same relation to Holland as before, Possession was therefore taken, not, however, without some opposition from the elder branch of the reigning family. Affairs remained on the same footing as before the capture of the island by the English, until the death of Tleman. His son Adam succeeded him, and is still in existence: he is well known for his kindness, and the favour he always shows to the Dutch. Fresh treaties were entered into with the prince, and amongst other stipulations, that of building another fort at Marabahan was determined upon. The relations between the prince and the Dutch are now said to be established on a durable footing. In 1773, the English formed an establishment on the island of Balambangan, off the N coast of B., which proved unsuccessful Disease and native treachery destroyed this settlement; and though it was re-established in 1802 by Lord Wellesley, when governor-general of India, it was withdrawn by orders from home. Subsequently, the temporary acquisition of Java, and of settlements on the coast of Sumatra, and on their cession to the Dutch, in 1824, the formation of Singapore settlement, diverted our attention from the more southern portions of this vast, populous, and fertile archipelago; but gradually the attempts of the Dutch, in violation of its treaty-stipulations with us, to monopolize its whole trade,—their monstrous and cruel wars against the natives of Sumatra,—their illegal impositions on our commerce with Java,—their formation of secret treaties with native princes, -and their exclusion of British trade from the Molucca or Spice islands,-were re-directing the views of our government to the S of Singapore, when the Chinese war broke out and exhibited the Indian archipelago in all its political importance. Useful as Singapore undoubtedly was to us during those hostilities, its position, it became obvious, was too far north; and the inconvenience of having, between it and China, clusters of the most valuable islands infested by pirates became very apparent. Mr. Brooke, too, who had, unaided, formed a settlement of his own at B., overthrowing usurpation, and raising legitimacy to its rightful throne, brought the importance of this island still more prominently before the British government. So, the Chinese war over, Captain Keppel was commissioned to chastise native piracy; which he did most effectually and gallantly. One step leads to another. Captain Bethune followed on a political mission to B.- Proper; Mr. Brooke was appointed confidential agent of government in the island; and Labuan was selected as a spot suitable for a British settlement. To such a settlement in the Indian archipelago a political objection, and that, under all the circumstances, not a very formidable one, has been raised out of the treaty of 1824 which regulates the relations of England and Holland in those seas. By the 12th art, of that treaty England engaged, "That no British establishment shall be made on the Caraman isles, or on the islands of Bantam, Bustany, Lingin, or on any of the other islands S of the Straits of Singapore." Hitherto, it has been argued by the Dutch, jealous of any interference in the trade of the archipelago, that the words in italics prevent our forming settlements in any of the islands between Singapore and the Moluccas; and even on Labuan. But this,—even reading the article isolated from the rest of the treaty,—is clearly too wide an interpretation; all that the words "or on any other islands S of the straits of Singapore" can be fairly taken to mean is, islands similar in geographical position and political bearings to the other islands expressly named; that is, to the other small islands adjacent to the Dutch Sumatra settlements and to Java. Moreover, as to Labuan, in geographical position it is 5° within the line of demarcation indicated by the most literal rendering of the article in question. Even B. itself is clearly exempt from the provisions of this treaty, for it lies neither to the N nor S of Singapore, but is intersected nearly in the middle by the parallel of that point. The Dutch government of Java are at present in possession of most of the W coast of B., and have united their posts there under the name of the residency of the W coast of B. A rough man, constructed principally from the observations of Muller, shows this residency as extending over nearly one-third of the island. In a previous section of this article on these regions we have given a sketch of the extent of the Dutch claims in this island. Many of these are quite untenable. But it has been justly observed, by a writer in the Morning Chronicle, that "the Dutch, with their usual vigour and sagacity, are turning to the best possible account that delicate forbearance and dignified reserve by which we have been hitherto withheld from making use of the most obvious and important privileges secured to us by the treaty of 1824. Certain disclosures have recently been made respecting a new code designed for the regulation of affairs, internal and external, of the Dutch East Indies. And from these we may confidently infer that all the efforts of the Netherlands government will be concentrated in order to effect its one great purpose of obstructing the growth of British influence in the Eastern archipelago. It is believed in Holland that the prosperity of that country rests entirely on its Oriental possessions; the value of which, it is supposed, would be diminished, if not altogether annihilated, by the intermixture of British settlements. Every imaginable artifice has therefore been resorted to in order to guard against our dreaded rivalry, and they have formed the extraordinary plan of opposing what they regard as our encroachments by the construction of that code of which we have already spoken. Its object is to place all but unlimited power for mischief in the hands of the governor-general, while his functions for good are circumscribed in the most remarkable manner. Events will soon force upon us a fresh adjustment of our mutual pretensions in the Indian archipelago. The subjects of the two countries will be established there in presence of each other, and they who display the greater energy, intelligence, and integrity, must triumph in the end. Hitherto the Netherlands authorities have regulated their conduct, as well towards us as towards the natives, by the most questionable maxims of morality; concluding treaties with Malay and other chiefs, carrying on destructive wars, and constantly forming fresh settlements in the teeth of their most solemn engagements with us. It was all along foreseen, however, that an end must sooner or later be put to such proceedings, and we are now apparently approaching the moment of reckoning. Every day augments the interest with which the public regard the Eastern archipelago, so that the feeling of the country would now go along with the Minister in whatever he might determine to do, of course consistently with justice, for the promotion of British commerce in that part of Asia." Borneo Proper, on the N side of the island, the Pho lo of the Chinese, has a sea-coast of more than 700 m. in extent, by a breadth of from 100 to 150 m. Berghaus estimates its area at 1,800 German or 38,223 English sq. m. It has the Dutch residency on the W; the boundary in this direction being Tanjong-Datoe, in 2° N lat., and 109° E long. On the E it has the state of Suluk; the mouth of the Sandahan river, or China-Batangan, being the frontier. The coast from Datoe to Sadong, an extent of about 100 m., including the district of Sarawak, is mountainous; from Sadong to Baram, about 250 m., it is flat; and thence to the N extremity of the state, at Malludu, in N lat. 7°, E long. 117°, it is mountainous. The whole coast is well-watered. Commencing at Datoe, we have the Landu; the Sarawak, with at least 12 mouths, and in which the tide reaches 45 m. from the sea; the Samarahan; the Sadong; the Batang-Lupar, a noble stream, 2 m. wide at the entrance, and for 20 m. very deep; the Seribas, Kalaka, Rejang, Palo, Bintalu, and Brune rivers. On the S it has various savage Dyak tribes, as the Dayan, Dusum, Murut, and Tataos. To this state belong the islands of Malaweli, Banggi, Balambangan, twice a British settlement, Balabak, and Babullan, containing several fine harbours, all favourably situated for the trade of China, the Philippines, and their own vicinity. The interior of this territory is filled by extensive chains of high mountains, the most elevated of which is Kinibalu, in 6° N lat., and visible from both sides of the island, which here runs out into a sort of peninsula. The western districts, as Sarawak and Kasinlaka, between 2° and 3° N lat., abound in metals, as gold, zinc, and antimony. Though land-animals are numerous, yet it is curious that the elephant, the rhinoceros, and a species of leopard, exist only in the northern peninsular extremity of this vast island, in the districts of Unsang and Paitna, and are nowhere to be found in any part of the archipelago to the eastward of this. The pop. is estimated by Berghaus at 900,000. Like all countries in a rude and unimproved state, this territory is inhabited, or rather infested, by numerous barbarous tribes, differing from each other in language, and ever in a state of hostility. The principal tribes inhabiting the country are 16 in number, of which the Malays are the chief, the most powerful, and the most civilized. The N part is partially occupied by squatters, a mixture of Arabs, Malays, Illanun, and Julak. For the last thirty years the country has been gradually deserted by the Chinese, who have been driven away by the exactions of the B. The only Chinese colony at present existing is at Sarawak. The Pagan tribes closely resemble each other in manners and customs, and in one thing most of them agree - in cutting off human heads and hoarding skulls. Savage, however, as they are, they are not, in some respects, in the lowest scale of social life, as they have almost all some knowledge of agriculture, cultivating rice and farinaceous roots and pulse. In religious feelings the Bornean savages are eminently deficient; they have no apparent system of religious belief, have neither gods nor idols, temples nor priests; yet they are superstitious, and attentive to good or bad omens, and especially to the cry of birds. None of them have any knowledge of an alphabet, or any means, by visible signs, of permanently recording their ideas: which is somewhat singular, as all the great islands have each one or more alphabets. This may be owing to the primitive sterility of a country rich in minerals but without foreign intercourse with people more civilized than themselves; and the difficulty of communication with a coast which has few large openings into the interior by means of bays, indentations, or large estuaries, but is throughout a compact and unbroken shore. A journey of 3 or 4 hours up the B. river brings you to a tract of country inhabited by a Dyak tribe. It appears that the intercourse between the Dyaks of this part of B., and the inhabitants of the country near the coast, is both rare and limited. They are regarded with dread as a race of ferocious savages, and with abhorrence as pagans or idolaters. The revolting passion for the possession of human heads, which is the well-known characteristic of the Dyak races, exists in perfection among this tribe. Every house is a complete Golgotha. In one house alone between 400 and 500 human heads were seen suspended from the ceiling! These Dyaks are strangers to fire-arms of every description, and exhibited the utmost astonishment on their being used. The sumpitan, or long pipe through which they blow, and with which they cast a short poisoned arrow with great force and precision, appears to be their favourite weapon. In an interesting notice of the island of B., contained in the Chinese Repository, it is stated that the Dyaks of Mampava "sometimes connect themselves with pirates, on the condition that the skulls and iron shall be theirs, and the other plunder go to the pirates." They would probably buy any skull that was offered; and no doubt many of these ornaments cost their possessors something very different from personal strife and hard blows. The Dyaks of B. Proper are excessively filthy in their habits. The stench within their houses is almost pestilential; pigs in great numbers are familiar inmates of their dwellings, as also dogs, goats, and monkeys, with other animals of a less domestic nature. They are, it appears, supposed to worship the pig-but they make no scruple in killing and eating it, as well as other animals, among which are rats and monkeys. The cow as well as the buffalo are common to this part of the country, and the Dyaks drink the milk of both. With respect to dress, the Dyak goes about nearly in a state of entire nudity, a strip of cloth round the loins being his only covering. The raiment of the women is not quite so scanty, and consists of a piece of cloth tied round the waist and reaching to the knees. The women are sometimes as expert as their husbands in the use of the blow-pipe, and have been known to take successful revenge for his death, at the hands of another. The men are addicted to smoking, and also distil a strong spirit from rice, resembling Hollands in appearance and partly in flavour, in which they lavishly indulge. They are not, however, so indolent as the Borneans; but work in the field and forests with their wives. The chase is a favourite amusement; and in this sport the sumpitan is the usual and most effective instrument of destruction. On festive occasions they have music and dancing; the men play and the women dance, while they, at the same time, accompany the music with their voices. The present sultan of B. is a Malay prince, whose Malay subjects do not constitute one-tenth of the population, even including the tribes converted to the faith of the Koran. Like the other Malayan races, they seem to have come from the interior of Sumatra to the W coast of Borneo, and thence to the N coast; and this is an event of no great antiquity. The government is, like that of other Malay states, hereditary and despotic. The radah or sultan has a council of four ministers,—the treasurer, the general, the chief justice, and the minister properly so called. Under these are two subordinate great officers, the second minister, and a deputy-general. The affairs of trade are managed by four inferior chiefs, of whom the principal are the intendant of the port and the warehouse-keeper, which latter was sent as an envoy to Singapore in 1825, to open up a commercial correspondence with the British government. There are in B. 30 or 40 pangerans, or hereditary governors, rendering the government a sort of aristocracy. The appearance and situation of the chief city of B. Proper are thus described by Mr. Brooke: "On approaching the town, before the ebb had run long, it appeared to be a very Venice of hovels, -a river Cybele, rising from the water. For those who like it, the locality is not ill chosen. The hills recede from the river and form an amphitheatre; and several other rivers or streams flowing in, cause a muddy deposit, on which the houses are built. At high water they are surrounded; at low water, stand on a sheet of mud. On nearing it, we were encompassed by boats which preceded and followed us, and we passed the floating market, where women, wearing immense hats of palm-leaves, sell all sorts of edibles, balanced in their little canoes, now giving a paddle, now making a bargain, and dropping down with the tide, and again regaining their place when the bargain is finished. The first impression of the town is miserable. The houses are crowded and numerous, and arriving at the palace does not present a more captivating aspect, for, though large, it is as incommodious as the worst." The houses, which are entirely composed of the nebong, kajang, and attap, are all built over the river, close to its banks, and supported on posts. In most of the accounts we have seen, the town is stated to be 10 m. up the river; but an article in the Singapore Free Press places it at about 4 m. from its embouchure, and describes it as extending altogether about 3 m. along the sides of the river. The houses are placed in rows, two and three deep,—the communication between them being entirely by water, except where, in some instances, planks are stretched across from one to the other. The pop. appears to be very considerable, probably not less than 100,000, of which 20,000 are slaves. The scenery, on entering the river, as well as in the neighbourhood of the town, is extremely beautiful. The river flows through a valley lying between two ridges of hills which descend to within a few miles of the town. These hills are not, -as is almost universally the case with all mountain scenery in this part of the world, -covered with forest from the base to the summit, but are wooded only about the lower slopes, while their sides are covered with a long grass, with trees occasionally interspersed. The river is about a mile broad where the town is placed; and the water is here always brackish from the influx of the tide; but fresh water is supplied in the utmost abundance, and of excellent quality, from wells and springs in the neighbourhood. The river is navigable by vessels of considerable burthen for some miles beyond the town; but it is thought prudent to remain outside at the usual anchorage, which is about 10 m. distant from it. Commerce.] The staple native exports of B. are camphor, which greatly excels that of Sumatra in quality, and of which about 35 peculs, of 125 lbs. each, were annually produced some years ago; pepper, the cultivation of which was introduced by the Chinese nearly a century ago, and of which 4,000 peculs were produced in 1774; tortoise shell, pearls, and mother-of-pearl; trepang, edible birds' nests, sago, dye-woods, and aromatic woods. It trades with China, the Sulu islands, the Philippines, the straits of Malacca, Singapore, and the W coast of B. itself. The trade with China was formerly much greater than it is at present. According to Forster, there were 7 junks in the river of B. in 1775. They chiefly imported nankeens and wrought silks. The trade with the Sulu islands has been frequently interrupted by the common piracies of both countries. With Manilla, a considerable trade was once conducted, the voyage not exceeding seven days; but this trade does not now exist. By far the most considerable trade is that now conducted with the British settlement of Singapore. In 1845-6, the total imports at that port from B. were valued at 10,92,204 rupees. The principal items were antimony ore, 20,190 peculs; betel nut, 2,441 peculs; edible birds' nests, 180 peculs black, and 6 peculs white; camphor, 21 peculs; pepper, 2,900 peculs; rattans, 20,006 peculs; sago, 180,400 bundles; bullion, 5,43,467 rupees. The import of gold-dust from B. in 1843-4 amounted to 144,334 dollars. The exports from Singapore to B. were in value 9,42,927 rupees. Amongst the principal items were bees' wax, 105 peculs; British cotton and woollen fabrics, 1,12,670 rupees; China crockery, 23,533 rupees; hardware, 14,690 rupees; bullion, 1,48,852 rupees. To these articles we fear must be added opium, to a large amount. The export of this article from Singapore to B., in 1843-4, amounted in value to 85,000 dollars; that of tobacco, in the same year, to 6,372 dollars. The usual method of conducting mercantile transactions at B. is by barter; and this principle extends itself to the most ordinary operations of domestic life. English bar iron, cut into pieces of about a finger's length, constitute the lowest standard of value, and a piece of blue Madras cloth, (salempores or moorees) circulate with the same freedom as a Spanish dollar at this place. Slaves are bought and sold for so many peculs of pepper, or so many catties of camphor; and a whole ship's cargo must be disposed of by the same process. 1 pecul of guns is equal to 30 dollars; 4 pieces of nankin = 1 dollar; 20 pieces of iron = \frac{1}{4} dollar. At Sarawak, 12 fanams = 1 Java rupee; 24 fanams = 1 real; 30 fanams = 1 dollar.—The chief article of subsistence among the people of B. Proper is sago, although rice, which is much dearer, is preferred, and sometimes used among the lower, and always among the wealthier classes of inhabitants; but, whether from possessing an abundant supply of an article of spontaneous growth and easy preparation like sago, from the unfavourable nature of the soil, or from the proverbial indolence of the inhabitants, there is no rice cultivation visible in the neighbourhood of the town. The chief supplies of this article appear to come from the interior, where its cultivation is more attended to by the Dyaks. It is brought down the river in husk, of which it is cleared, in the method usual among the Malays, by the slaves. No inconsiderable proportion of the property of the more affluent class consists of slaves. The sultan owns 500 or 600 male and female; and several of his nobles, or chief officers of state, possess nearly an equal number. Most of the slaves imported, are brought from the islands to he eastward. Many of them have been captured by pirates; but, from the number of women among them, it would appear as if a regular slave trade was carried on with these islands. They are, of course, valued variously; but for a young active lad, 31 catties of camphor, of which the price is 12 dollars a catty, have been taken. Female slaves, if young and handsome, are promoted to the harem,—while domestic drudgery becomes the lot of the less attractive. But in B. Proper all work is performed by the women - the "lords of the creation" there think every occupation, besides smoking tobacco, quite beneath them, and they never go out of doors except for the sake of amusement, to make a bargain, or perhaps to go to mosque. The men, however, are not totally uninstructed; a considerable majority of them know to read or write, and are conversant with the Malayan as well as the Bornean languages. The chiefs and men of rank pique themselves on speaking a very pure and high Malay. The chiefs or nobles of B. are unfortunately numerous, and they are sometimes able to exercise a most injurious influence over the operations of commerce, which they would fain reserve altogether for themselves. The nakhodas, or principal men among the trading class, are deservedly preferred, and are said to be very fair in all their dealings. There appears to be no fixed rate of duty at B. on the importation or exportation of goods, which is at the discretion of the sultan. When an English vessel arrives to trade, the amount of the duty to be charged is settled between the nakhodas and the sultan: the duty is always laid on the produce exported, and is paid to the sultan in kind; and he generally barters it again with the supercargo for the same goods as the nakhodas have been buying from him. It appears that the Chinese in this part of the island of B. are far from being so numerous as in other parts of it; they are assembled at a short distance from the town in a small kampong, or village, which does not altogether contain more than thirty of them. It is probable they were formerly more numerous, when the junk trade from China with B. Proper was more considerable than it is at present. There are no artificers among those now there, who are solely engaged in the cultivation of pepper and articles. No mines have been opened by them in this part of the country. History.] Recent events have rendered this state an object of importance to Britain: a Mr. Brooke, an English gentleman of fortune, having, by an extraordinary union of personal courage, political sagacity, and patriotic and philanthropical sentiments, succeeded in a well-conceived project for opening up the rich islands of the Indian archipelago to British commerce, and introducing into these vast and populous regions the blessings of civilization and Christianity. Mr. Brooke having manned a large yacht with a choice crew, sailed from England, for the Indian archipelago, in 1838, and arrived in the Sarawak river, on the NW coast of B., at a time when there existed a civil war between the Malay rajah of Sarawak and his subjects. This chief—who has since become known to Europe under the name of Muda Hassim—immediately understood the extraordinary advantages he might derive from the arrival of his European guest; on the other hand, Mr. Brooke also perceived at once how useful the actual posture of affairs might prove in the furtherance of his own designs. He, therefore, listened to the overtures made to him by Muda Hassim, and consented to lend his assistance in putting down the rebellien, provided that such of the insurgent chiefs and their followers as might be reduced to obedience by his aid should be treated with justice and humanity. Into the details of this extraordinary campaign it is unnecessary to enter, since the whole history of it has been given to the public in Mr. Brooke's own language. The insurrection was suppressed, and shortly afterwards the Malay chief, on proceeding to the capital to take upon himself the duties of minister to the sultan, relinquished the government of the province, with the title of rajah, to Mr. Brooke. We now behold him, therefore, raised by a course of circumstances, by which he knew how to profit, to the rank of a prince, the extent of whose territories it was not easy to define; for although the length of Sarawak on the sea-coast be limited, it depends entirely on Mr. Brooke's own moderation what frontier to assign to it inland. Mr. Brooke himself perfectly well understood the value of his acquisition. He saw himself placed in possession of an extensive jungle, thinly sprinkled with human habitations, and those rude and imperfect clearances which may be denominated Dyak farms; and he knew that it would depend entirely on himself whether the population of his dominions increased or diminished. For some years he stood exposed to the most imminent peril, as well from the illregulated passions of his Malay and Dyak subjects, as from the ferocity and cupidity of piratical hordes, who subsist on the plunder of trade and kidnapping of prisoners. He could, in fact, be said to have no safety at all, till the policy of the British government granted him, under certain limitations, the protection of the squadron stationed in the China seas. Until then he had to entirely rely on his own courage and intellectual resources. Yet, under these disadvantages, he succeeded in implanting the first seeds of civilization among the Dyaks, taught them some of the most obvious benefits of order and subordination, and, by the mildness of his sway, caused himself to be beloved, though in many respects exercising despotic authority. Of course his great aim has been to concentrate in his territory an industrious population, to give an impetus to agriculture, to invest commerce with respect, and to excite in the Dyaks those wants which constitute the first impulse to all human improvement. The rude people have been taught the value of intercommunication, have been incited to construct bridges, and lay down primitive roads for their own use, which they do by felling trees, and forming with them a pathway through the jungle, or over swamps and morasses, and to clear fresh farms for the planting of corn, cotton, or other useful products. One of the most profitable occupations to which the Dyaks have been induced to apply themselves by Mr. Brooke, is the planting of cocoa-tree groves, which has recently afforded employment to great numbers of that active and docile race. The jungle is cut down or burned, the land cleared and prepared, and the young plants, furnished by the rajah, are laid skilfully in the soil; then, with a power of vegetation unknown elsewhere, perhaps in the world, the young grove shoots up, and in a short time will produce its owner the most ample returns. Sarawak, the capital of the province, is springing rapidly into importance; new houses are constantly erected, new gardens and plantations laid out; while fresh accessions to the population are made from the country. One striking proof of their confidence in their new ruler is deserving of public notice. From time immemorial it had been customary among the Dyaks of Sarawak to erect their villages in the most inaccessible places, on the peaks of conical hills, in the depths of forests, on the crests of ridges, that they might thus, if possible, be safe from the sudden attack of enemies. They are now gradually leaving these fastnesses and building their dwellings in the open plain, conceiving the power and authority of their rajah a better protection than scarped cliffs or dense masses of jungle. [Abridged from Bentley's Miscellany.] In 1847, Mr. Brooke negotiated a treaty of friendship and commerce between the queen of England and the sultan of B., in which it is provided, 1. That peace, friendship, and good understanding shall for ever subsist between the two powers of Britain and B. 2. That the subjects of her majesty shall have full liberty to enter into, reside in, and trade with, all parts of the dominions of the sultan Omar Ali Saifadeen, and enjoy the privileges and advantages of the most favoured nation; the subjects of the sultan to enjoy the same privileges in this country. 3. That British subjects may purchase and acquire all kinds of property within the dominions of the sultan, his highness engaging that such British subjects shall, as far as lies in his power, enjoy full and complete protection and security. 4. That the trade between the two countries shall be perfectly free, subject only to the customs' duties which may be hereafter enforced. 5. That no duty exceeding one dollar per ton shall be levied on British vessels entering the port of B.; British goods and British trade to be exempt from all internal duties, &c. 6. That no export duties shall be levied by the sultan on any article the growth or produce of his own dominions. 7. That the ships of war of England and the India company shall be permitted to enter the ports, rivers, and creeks of his dominions, and to provide themselves with such supplies, stores, and provisions as they may require. 8. That assistance shall be rendered to vessels wrecked on the coast of B. 9. That every means shall be taken by both the high contracting parties for the suppression of piracy within the seas, straits, and rivers subject to their respective control or influence, &c. Article 10 confirms the cession of the island of Labuan, situate on the NW coast of B., together with the adjacent islets of Kuraman, Little Rusakan, Great Rusakan, Da-at, and Malankasan, and all the straits, islets, and seas situate half-way between the afore-mentioned islets and the mainland of B.; likewise, the distance of 10 geog. m. from the island of Labuan, to the W and N, and from the nearest point half-way between the islet of Malankansan and the mainland of B., in a line running N till it intersects a line extended from W to E, from a point 10 m. N at the northern extremity of the island of Labuan, to be possessed in perpetuity and full sovereignty by her majesty and her successors; and, to avoid disputes, the sultan engages not to make any similar cession of territory to any other nation without the consent of her Britannic majesty. Every measure is to be taken by both parties for the suppression of the abominable traffic in slaves. Authorities.] Beckmann's Voyage, 8vo., Lond., 1788.—Valentyn Beschryving von Borneo.—Raffles' Account. - Sonnerat's Notices. - Low's Sarawak. - Marryatt's B. and the Indian Archipelago.—Temminck's Coup d'Œil general sur les Possessions Neerlandaises. -Brooke's Narrative.

BORNHEM, a town of Belgium, in the prov. and 12 m. SSW of Antwerp, cant. of Puers, near the r. bank of the Scheldt. Pop. 4,043. It contains manufactories of linen, delft-ware, and oil; and has several bleacheries and rope-works.
BORNHOFT, or BORNHOVED, a town of Denmark, in the duchy of Holstein, 18 m. S of Kiel. Waldemar II. was defeated here in 1227 by Adolphus, count of Holstein.

BORNHOLM, a large island in the Baltic, belonging to Denmark Proper. It lies between the parallels of 55° 17' 30" and 54° 59' 15" N; and 2° 08' and 2° 35' E long. of Copenhagen [Baggesen]; 25 m. SE by E of the SE point of Sweden; 53 m. from the NE point of the island of Rugen; and 91 m. E of Stevnsklint, the nearest point in Seeland. Its greatest length from NW to SE, between Sandhammeren and Dueodde, is 24 m.; and its greatest breadth, from W by S to E by N, between Rönne and Svanike, 18 m.; but its general outline is rhomboidal, with an average length of 20 m., and average breadth of 11 m. Its superficial area is about 220 sq. m. Its coast-line is high and rocky, and bordered by dangerous reefs and shoals. About 1 m. SW from its N point, or Cape Hammeren, is a lighthouse, 280 ft. above sea-level, and visible in clear weather at 23 m. distance. The position of the flag-staff on Hammeren, according to British charts, is 55° 17′ 39" N lat., and 14° 46′ 13" E long. The island is intersected by a range of hills running N and S, and attaining an alt. in the hill called Rytterknägten of 496 ft. This, the highest point in the island, lies near its centre, and to the N of Aakirkeby. The Lyngmark, an extensive barren heath, occupies the centre of the island. The two principal streams are the Lesaue in the S, and the Baggeaue in the N part of the island. The soil is in general shallow and stony, but moderately fertile; some districts are covered with drift-sand. This sand is composed of exceedingly fine particles, which, in certain places, when the waves roll in with great violence against the strand, is washed up in large quantities; and when a high wind occurs, it is carried along like a snow-drift and thus collected in different localities. Dr. Forchhammer is of opinion that this island is being gradually elevated above sea-level, at the rate of about a foot in a century. The first beach formed upon it is now at a height of about 40 ft. Freestone, limestone, a species of dark blue marble, various species of clay, some of which are suitable for pottery, ochre, iron, slate, and coal, are wrought on the island. The climate is cold, but dry and salubrious. The vegetable productions are oats, barley, rye, pease, hemp, flax, and potatoes. The only large timber upon the island is that which is occasionally found embedded in its mosses. The cattle and horses are of a smaller breed than those of the larger Danish islands. Poultry is extensively reared; but game is scarce. Cod, salmon, haddock, and herring are fished upon the coast. About 100 tons of salted cod are annually exported.—The pop. in 1801 was 18,902; in 1846, 26,600, in 5,450 families. This pop. is of Danish extraction; and the language spoken is Danish with a considerable infusion of German words. The chief occupations of the pop. are agriculture and fishing. The freeholds of 8 tons of hardcorn and upwards, according to the standard by BOR which the different taxes upon landed property are computed and levied in Denmark—the Danish acre, or ton of land, being an area of 14,000 sq. ells of 2 ft., and from 10 to 25 such acres being reckoned to each ton of hardcorn, in proportion to the quality of the soil-was 49 in 1838; under 8 and above 2 tons, 26; from 2 to 1 ton, 21. The number of farms on long lease, of 8 tons and upwards, 527, of which the average size seems to have been 101 tons; under 8 tons, 1,205, of which the average size was 11 ton. The small farms were 690, amounting to only 110 tons in all. A considerable quantity of home-grown flax and wool is spun and manufactured by the females. Tile and brick-making, and the manufacture of pottery-ware, employ several hands; and, in the villages, clocks and watches are extensively manufactured for exportation.-The administrative division of the island is into four hardes or districts: viz. Wester-harde, comprising the town of Ronne, the cap. of the island, and 5 parishes; Norder-h., comprising the town of Hasle, the villages of Allinge and Sandvig, and 6 parishes; Oster-h., comprising the town of Svanike, and 5 parishes; and Suder-h., comprising the towns of Nexoe, Aakirkeby, and 6 parishes. -Rönne, in 55° 5′ 50" N lat., has a pop. of 4,300. About 5 leagues to the E of the N end of B. lie a cluster of islets, called the Ertholmes, consisting of Christiansholm, or Christiansoe, Friedrichsholm, and Gräsholm. They enclose a spacious and important harbour, with 3 fath. water, and large enough to contain 40 vessels; and are strongly fortified. On Christiansoe, the eastern of the group, is a revolving light, 96 ft. above sea-level. This light is in N lat. 55° 19' 19", E long. 15° 11′ 39".
BORNO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 32 m. NE of Bergamo, on the r. bank of the Oglio.
BORNOA, a river of Spain, in New Castile, which takes its rise in the Sierra Pela, on the N frontiers of the prov. of Guadalaxara, and, running S, unites with the Henares, 2 m. WSW of Jadraque.
BORNOS, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 36 m. ENE of Cadiz, on the r. bank of the Guadalete, 7 m. NE of Arcos. Pop. 4,000.
BORNSTADT, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, in the regency and about a mile NW of Potsdam. Pop. 500. It contains extensive breweries and distilleries.
BORNSTATT, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of Rems and Fils, 5 m. NNW of Ulm.

BORNU. An extensive kingdom in the E part of Central Africa, comprehended between the parallels of 10° and 15° N, and the meridian of 12° and 18° E. On the N it is bounded by Kanem, and the SE corner of the desert of Bilma; on the E by Lake Tchad; on the SE by the kingdom of Loggun, and the Shary river, which divides it from Bagarmeh; on the S it has Mandara; and on the W, Houssa. The Mahommedans were at one time accustomed to rank B. as the fourth state in power and extent in the world; and until the Fellatah conquest, its sultan appears to have been the most powerful sovereign in the interior of Africa: Bergu, Bagarmeh, Wangara, and Cassena, being tributary states to him. Berghaus estimates the area at 14,000, and Ungewitter at 16,000 German sq. m. These approximations appear to us to err by excess. Perhaps 160,000 sq. m. is the utmost territorial extent of the state of B. as now existing. General physical features.] The prevailing character of this country is that of a rich level surface, in which the undulations are scarcely perceptible to the eye. It forms in fact the great alluvial plain of Sudan, of which the prevailing slope is towards the great lake in its NE extremity, which may be supposed to form the drain of its retreating waters. Much of the country is covered with wood of a very close growth, intersected only by narrow paths along which travellers must proceed in file. The soil is in general a rich alluvial dark-coloured clay. The rise of the country is towards Mandara; but it does not appear that any portion of it stretches to the picturesque and apparently interminable chain of hills which marks the N frontier of Mandara.—The great physical feature of B. is Lake Tchad, the shores of which have not yet been fully explored by any European traveller. It was first beheld by Dr. Oudney and his companions on their reaching Lari, the frontier-town of Bornu, situated in about 14° 20' N lat., and nearly on the same meridian with Murzuk. "The soil near the edges of the lake was a firm dark mud; and in proof of the great overflowings and recedings of the waters, even in this advanced dry season, the stalks of the gussub of the preceding year were standing in the lake, more than 40 yards from the shore. The water is sweet and pleasant, and abounds with fish." After eight days' travelling along the W shore of the lake, they came to another important feature,—the river Yeou, a very considerable stream, rising in Haussa, flowing from the W, and falling into the Tchad. It was about 50 yards broad, and two canoes lay on the bank to ferry over goods and passengers. These vessels, though large, were rudely constructed of planks fastened together with cords: they received 20 or 30 persons, while the camels and horses swam with their heads made fast to the boats. The Shary flows from the S, through Loggun, and enters the SW extremity of the great lake in a noble stream, half-a-mile broad, and flowing with considerable rapidity. The Tchad may be about 200 m. in length, and 150 m. in breadth; but it has not yet been ascertained how far it extends towards the NE. It thus forms one of the largest bodies of fresh water in the world, though it cannot rival the mighty inland seas of Asia. Its dimensions vary in an extraordinary manner according to the season. An extent of many miles, usually dry, is submerged during the rains. This inundated tract, covered with impenetrable thickets, and with rank grass twice the human height, is unfit for the residence of men, and becomes a huge den of wild beasts; elephants of enormous dimensions, beneath whose reclining bodies large shrubs, and even young trees were seen crushed; lions, panthers, leopards, large flocks of hyenas, and snakes of monstrous bulk. It is a disastrous era when the returning waters dislodge these monsters of the wood, and drive them to seek their prey among the habitations of men. At this period travellers, and the persons employed in watching the harvests, often fall victims; nay, the hyenas have been known to carry walled towns by storm, and devour the herds which had been driven into them for shelter. Climate.] Major Denham considers the climate of B. quite as salubrious as that of any other country of the torrid zone, and preferable to many. The variation of the barometer is almost imperceptible; but the heat, as might be expected, is intense. The mean annual temp. is 83° 6'. From March to the end of June, the therm. at 2 P. M. will sometimes rise to 105° | bearing a nut of which both the kernel and shell are and 107°, and suffocating winds from the S and SE then prevail. The nights are also dreadfully oppressive. Violent tempests of lightning, thunder, and rain, occur towards the middle of May; and before the end of June large tracts of country are converted into lakes. In October the winter-season commences, with breezes from the NW. In December and January the therm. never exceeds 75° and sometimes | Mandara; and the date-tree occurs 4 days N of Kuka. sinks to 58°. The rainy period continues from 3 to 9 successive days, with short intervals from the occasional shifting of the wind to the N or W. During this period, the inhabitants confine themselves closely to their dwellings; but the rest of the first season, however sultry or wet, does not suspend the labour of the fields. With the commencement of the second season, towards the end of October, the ardent heat subsides,-the air becomes mild,-the weather continues serene, -and, as the year declines, the mornings before sunrise are unpleasantly cool. Soil and agriculture.] The soil of B. is, in general, amazingly fertile, though frequently interrupted by stripes of barren sand. The grain principally cultivated is Indian corn, of two different kinds, distinguished by the names of the gassob or gussob, and the gamphuly. The gassob-which, in its general shape, resembles the common reed-is of two species; the first grows with a long stalk that bears an ear from 8 to 12 inches long, and containing, in little husks or cavities, from 300 to 500 grains of the size of small pease. The second species, which is common in Tripoli, differs from the first only in the shorter size of its ear. The stalk of the gamphuly is much thicker than that of the gassob; its ears are more numerous, for it has several on the same reed; and the size of its grain is considerably larger. This is the same kind of grain which is frequently seen in Spain, and is there called maize. Wheat and barley are not raised in B.; but the horse-bean of Europe, and the common kidney-bean, are cultivated with much assiduity as they are used for food both to the slaves and to the cattle. Gum-trees are thinly scattered throughout the country. Cotton, hemp, and indigo, are produced in great abundance. In the agriculture of B. the plough is unknown, and the hoe is the only instrument employed. In the labours of husbandry, the men are always assisted by the women. While the former open the ground with their hoes, and form the trenches in straight parallel lines, the women follow and throw in the seed; and as soon as the weeds begin to rise, it is the business of the women to root them up with the hoe. The sowing-season commences when the periodical rains of April have ceased; and so rapid in this climate is the vegetation, that the gassob is reaped early in July; but the gamphuly, of slower growth, is seldom cut down till the month of August or September. Besides the vegetable productions already mentioned, two species of roots are used by the inhabitants of B., which constitute a wholesome and substantial food. The one called dondu, produces a low plant, with branches that spread 4 or 5 feet upon the ground, and leaves resembling those of the garden-bean. In five months after it has been planted, the leaves fall off, and the root is taken from the ground, and, being cut into small pieces, is dried in the sun, in which state it may be kept for two years. Before being used as food, it is reduced to a fine powder, and mixed with palm-oil till it assumes the consistency of paste. The other root is that of a tree with the name of which we are unacquainted: it is prepared for use by boiling, without any further process. There is a valuable tree called the kedeynah, indigenous, and, as far as we know, peculiar to this country, in form and height resembling the olive and the lemon in its leaf, and in great estimation, the first as a fruit, the last on account of the oil extracted from it, which the people of B. burn in their lamps as a substitute for the oil of olives. Cotton and indigo are grown; but in this fine climate, there is not a vegetable raised except the onion; and B. is almost entirely destitute of fruittrees. A few mangoes are seen near the frontiers of B., especially on the shore of the Tchad, and along the Shary, abounds in domestic animals, such as cows, sheep, goats, horses, camels, and buffaloes. Bullocks are the medium of commerce for every thing; from 100 to 150 of these animals are given for a good horse. Abundance of fowls are reared, and hives of bees are everywhere seen. Lions, leopards, civet-cats, small wolves, foxes, wild dogs, monkeys, giraffes, and an animal called kurigum, about the size of a red-deer, with annulated horns, are enumerated as belonging to the zoology of B. Elephants are sometimes seen, but are not numerous; in the rivers are found the crocodile and hippopotamus, of both of which the flesh is eaten. The country is much infested with reptiles, particularly snakes and scorpions. Among the game may be enumerated many species of antelopes, partridges, snipes, cranes, wild ducks, and ostriches. The flesh of the ostrich is here considered a luxury. "The cattle are bred in vast herds by an Arab tribe called Shouaas, who have transported into B. all the pastoral habits of their nation. Sometimes the daughter or the wife of a rich Shouaa will be mounted on her particular bullock, and precede the loaded animals,-extravagantly adorned with amber, silver rings, coral, and all sorts of finery, her hair streaming with fat, a black rim of kohol, at least an inch wide, round each of her eyes, and, I may say, arrayed for conquest at the crowded market. Carpets or tobes are then spread on her clumsy palfrey: she sits on her jambe decà jambe dela, and with considerable grace guides her animal by his nose. Notwithstanding the peaceableness of his nature, her vanity still enables her to torture him into something like caperings and curvetings." [Denham.] Inhabitants, Manners and Customs.] The pop. of B. is said to be very great. Within the bounds of the empire, we are informed, that no less than 30 languages are spoken; but this, though it were true, is not an unequivocal proof of the number of inhabitants. Major Denham estimated the pop. at 5,000,000. This is of course a mere approximation, and probably an over-estimate; but we have no materials on which to found any other estimate. The complexion of the Bornuese, or Kanury as they call themselves, is entirely black; but their features are different from the general features of Negroes. The women are cleanly, but not good-looking. Both sexes are distinguished by high foreheads. Major Denham confesses that after entering the Negro country, his eye soon became quite reconciled to the complexion of the women,-"the shadowed livery of the burning sun." The following paragraph occurs in his work: "Though many degrees fairer, and nearer our own blue-eyed beauties in complexion, when moderately cleansed and washed, yet no people ever lost more by comparison than did the white ladies of Murzuk with the black ones of B. and Sudan. That the latter were black there is no denying; but their beautiful forms, expressive eyes, pearly teeth, and excessive cleanliness, rendered them far more pleasing than the dirty half-castes we were now amongst." The dress consists of a robe of blue cotton-cloth, a turban formed of folds of cotton-cloth brought from Cairo, and a red cap from Tripoli. Among the lower ranks, a girdle round the waist sometimes constitutes the only covering. Ben Ali informs us, that the principal people are distinguished by rings of gold worn in the nose. The tattooing common to all Negro nations in these latitudes, is here particularly unbecoming. They have 20 cuts or lines on each side of the face, drawn from the corner of the mouth towards the angles of the lower jaw and cheek-bone. They have also 1 cut in the centre of the forehead, 6 on each arm, 6 on each leg and thigh, 4 on each breast, and 9 on each side just above the hips. The Shouaa Arabs are a fine race of men, bearing scarcely any resemblance to the Northern Arabs, but strikingly resembling, in features as well as habits, the best favoured English gypsies. They speak a pure dialect of Egyptian Arabic. Among the lower orders of the people, drafts is the common amusement, as chess is among the nobles: at both games they are very expert. They are dexterous spearmen. Denham saw one of their chiefs, in battle with the Fellatahs, throw eight spears, which all *old at the distance of 30 or 35 yards.—The mode of building resembles that which is common among the Moors of Barbary. Four walls, enclosing a square, are first erected. Within these, and parallel to them, are built four other walls; the intervening space is then divided into different apartments, and covered with a roof. Thus the space within the interior walls determines the size of the court; the space between the walls determines the width of the apartments; and the rooms are of the same height as the walls. On the outside of the house there is usually a second square or large yard, surrounded by a wall, for the accommodation of the cattle. In the construction of the walls, the following method is invariably adopted: A trench being made for the foundation, is filled with dry and solid materials, rammed in with force, and levelled; over these is placed a layer of tempered mud or clay, in which are regularly fixed a proper number of stones. Thus with alternate layers of clay and stones, the wall is raised to the height of 6 or 7 ft., when the workmen suspend its progress for a week that it may have time to settle and become compact; for which purpose they water it every day. The roofs are formed of branches of the palm-tree, intermixed with brushwood, and covered with layers of earth, in such a manner as at first to be water proof, though the violence of the wind and rain generally destroys them before the end of the second year. The whole building is white-washed with a species of chalk. Though the symmetry of the houses, and their general resemblance to each other, would easily have admitted of a regular arrangement of streets, yet all the towns of B. consist of houses straggling wide of each other, and placed without method or rule. "The obvious propriety of giving to the principal mosque a central situation, exhibits the only proof of attention to general convenience." The articles of furniture are mean, and few in number. In the houses are generally found mats covered with sheep-skins, for a bed; earthen pots and pans, several wooden dishes and bowls, a carpet, a lamp for oil, and sometimes a copper kettle. To these articles the higher ranks add handsome carpets, leathern cushions stuffed with wool, several utensils of copper and brass, with a kind of candlestick in which they burn candles made of tallow and bees' wax. "The fashions of courts," says a lively reviewer, "are often little under the guidance either of nature or taste; nor has Europe in this respect always ground to reproach the rest of the world: but there is probably no court of which the taste is so absurd, grotesque, or preposterous, as that of B. A huge belly is considered the primary requisite of a fine gentleman, or of one fit to wait on the sultan; and where feeding and cramming will not produce this elegant feature, the part is cushioned and stuffed out till it appears to possess the required dimension. The honour attached to this form must arise seemingly from its being considered as a type of abundance and luxury. Over this unwieldy bulk are then thrown ten or twelve successive robes of various and rich materials. The head, too, is covered with fold over fold, till there is seen only a small part of the face, which, according to the nicest taste, ought to appear entirely on one side. Over all are numberless charms enclosed in green leather cases, covering their clothes, horses, and arms. In this attire these champions actually take the field; but the idea of such unwieldy hogsheads acting any part in battle, appeared to the mission utterly ridiculous." Military force.] The sheikh's force was composed of 30,000 cavalry and 9,000 Kanembu infantry, when Denham visited this country. In approaching Kouka, the capital, Denham and his party were totally ignorant of the character or condition of the people they were about to visit. They had heard different reports of the military force of the sovereign of B. One said they were a few ragged Negroes armed with spears,—others that his forces were numerous and well-trained. They knew not what credit to attach to those different reports, and were in doubt whether they should find a chief at the head of thousands, or be received under a tree by a barbarian surrounded by a few naked slaves. The real state of the case is described in the following passage: "These doubts, however, were quickly removed. I had ridden on a short distance in front of Boo-Khaloom—an Arab merchant by whom the expedition was accompanied—with his train of Arabs all mounted and dressed out in their best apparel, and from the thickness of the trees soon lost sight of them. I rode still onwards, and, on approaching a spot less thickly planted, was not a little surprised to see in front of me a body of several thousand cavalry drawn up in line, and extending right and left quite as far as I could see; and, checking my horse, I awaited the arrival of my party, under the shade of a wide-spreading acacia. The B. troops remained quite steady, without noise or confusion: and a few horsemen, who were moving about in front giving directions, were the only persons out of the ranks. On the Arabs appearing in sight, a shout, or yell was given by the sheikh's people, which rent the air; a blast was blown from their rude instruments of music equally loud, and they moved on to meet Boo-Khaloom and his Arabs. There was an appearance of tact and management in their movements which astonished me; three separate small bodies, from the centre and each flank, kept charging rapidly towards us, to within a few feet of our horses' heads, without checking the speed of their own until the moment of their halt, while the whole body moved onwards. These parties were mounted on small but very perfect horses, who stopped and wheeled from their utmost speed with great precision and expertness, shaking their spears over their heads, exclaiming 'Barca! Barca! Alla niakkum cha, alla cheraga! 'Blessing, blessing, Sons of your country! Sons of your country!' and returning quickly to the front of the body, in order to repeat the charge. While all this was going on, they closed in their right and left flanks, and surrounded the little body of Arab warriors so completely, as to give the compliment of welcoming them very much the appearance of a declaration of a contempt for their weakness." Language and Religion.] The languages spoken in B., as already mentioned, are numerous. The Arabic language is understood, and is used in conversation by the principal families. The Arabic characters are used in writing the language of B.—The Bornouese are strict Moslem, and less tolerant than the Arabs. "I have known a Bornouese," says Denham, "refuse to eat with an Arab, because he had not performed sully, that is, washed and prayed at the preceding appointed hour. In the towns are many hajis who have performed the pilgrimage to Mecca, and who excel in writing the Arabic characters, as well as in teaching the art to others. However strange it may appear, each kafila leaving B. for Fezzan, carries several copies of the Koran written by the B. fighis, which will sell in Barbary or Egypt for 40 or 50 dollars each. The Arabic characters are also used by them to express their own language. Every chief has one of these fighis attached to him, who writes despatches from his dictation with great facility." Commerce and Manufactures. Instead of grain, as in Fezzan, the medium of exchange, with regard to smaller articles, in B., is a kind of metal which seems to be a mixture of copper and brass, and is formed into pieces of different weights. The principal articles which B. furnishes are, gold-dust, slaves, cattle, horses, ostrich-feathers, salt, and civet. We are not informed whether the gold-dust be a native product, or procured by trading with other nations. The slaves are procured chiefly from Bagarmeh and other districts to the S and W of B., where a regular system of slave-hunting has long been carried on. The inhabitants of these districts make yearly incursions into the neighbouring Negro countries, and carry away as slaves as many of the inhabitants as they can make prisoners. The civet is obtained from a species of cat. The animal is irritated till it perspire copiously, and then the moisture is carefully scraped from the hair: this operation, it is said, is renewed at short intervals, till the animal expire, which usually happens in twelve or fourteen days. One cat generally yields half-an-ounce of civet. The inhabitants of B. form the iron afforded by their country into such slight tools as their work requires. Working in iron, among nations whose chiefs at least are martial, has usually got a start beyond other arts; but when an English carpenter undertook to repair a small fieldpiece, and obtained for that purpose the assistance of the best B. workmen, he was kept in a state of perpetual agony by the clumsiness with which they handled their tools. The only manufacture in which they have attained to any kind of excellence, is that of cotton cloth dyed blue with their fine indigo, the tobes or pieces of which are the current coin of the realm; and yet in this staple fabric of Central Africa, they are much excelled by the people of Nigritia, and those to the south. Of their hemp, they make a kind of coarse linen; and of cotton, a kind of cloth which is dyed blue, and highly valued. They manufacture carpets for coverings to their horses; and coverings for tents, of wool and the hair of camels and goats. But none of these manufactures are so extensive as to allow of exportation. In return for the articles exported, they receive copper, brass, and dollars from Tripoli, by the way of Fezzan, and red woollen caps, check linens, light coarse woollen cloths, baize, baracans, small Turkey carpets, plain Mesurata carpets, silk wrought and unwrought, tissues and brocades, sabre-blades, knives, scissars, coral beads, small looking-glasses, &c. Towns. The towns of B. are in general large and well-built, and surrounded with walls from 35 to 40 ft. in height, and 20 ft. thick. The principal, besides Kouka, are New Birnie, the residence of the sultan,- Old Birnie, the ancient capital, which is said to have had once a population of 200,000 souls, no longer exists,-and Angornu, 16 m. from Kouka, with 30,000 inhabitants. History.] It has been suggested by Mr. Leake [Geog. Journ. ii.] that B. is the ancient Agisymba to which Julius Maternus I. penetrated after a march of four months from Leptis Magna as related by Ptolemy on the authority of Marinus of Tyre. B. is described by Leo, who visited Nigritia in the 15th cent., as an extensive territory to the W of Wankarah, governed by a powerful Libyan or Berber prince. Achmet Ali, who occupied the throne in 1808, is said to have been descended from a royal line of ancestors. He contended for several years against the rising power of the Felatahs of Sudan; but was at length overcome and deprived of his possessions. The Felatahs, however, did not long retain possession of the territory. Shortly after the conquest, sheikh Alameen el Kanemy, who now holds the reins of sovereignty, formed the project of delivering the country from the bondage into which it had fallen. Born in Fezzan, of Kanembu parentage, though, on the father's side, descended from a Moor, Alameen had, after visiting Egypt, proceeded to Kanem as sheikh of the Koran; where, by the correctness of his life and the benevolence of his disposition, he made himself greatly respected and beloved. The miracles and cures which he wrought by writing charms, became the theme of the surrounding country: in fact, he became invested with all the mysterious influence of a Maraboot. Having stirred up the Kanembus to assist him, by means of a well-planned tale of having been called by a vision to the patriotic enterprise, he made his first campaign with scarcely 400 followers, at the head of whom he defeated an army of the Felatahs nearly 8,000 strong. This victory he followed up with great promptitude and resolution; and in less than ten months he had been the conqueror in forty different battles. "Nature," says Denham, "had bestowed on him all the qualifications for a great commander; an enterprising genius, sound judgment, features engaging, with a demeanour gentle and conciliating. And so little of vanity was mixed with his ambition, that he refused the offer of being made sultan; but, placing Mohammed, the brother of sultan Achmet, on the throne, he, first doing homage himself, insisted on the whole army following his example. The sheikh built for sultan Mohammed his present residence, New Birnie, establishing himself at Angornu, 3 m. distant, and retaining the dictatorship of the kingdom pro tempore. Such a commencement was also extremely politic on the part of the sheikh; but his aspiring mind was not calculated to rest satisfied with such an arrangement. The whole population now flocked to his standard, and appeared willing to invest him with superior power, and a force to support it. One of the first offers they made, was to furnish him with twenty horses per day, until a more regular force was organized, which continued for four years. He now raised the green flag, the standard of the Prophet; refused all titles but that of the 'Servant of God;' and after clearing the country of the Felatahs, he proceeded to punish all those nations who had given them assistance; and with the slaves, the produce of these wars, rewarded his faithful Kanembus and other followers for their fidelity and attachment. Even in the breasts of some of the Bornouese, successful war had raised a passion for conquest: their victories not less a matter of surprise than delight, crest-fallen and dispirited as they were, gave a stimulus to their exertions, and they became accustomed to warfare and regardless of danger. If he has impressed his followers with a belief that supernatural powers are vested in their leader, much good policy as well as superstition may have influenced his conduct. No one could have used greater endeavours to substitute laws of reason for practices of barbarity; and, though feared, he is loved and respected. 'When lenity and cruelty play for a kingdom, the gentlest gamester is the soonest winner.' Compared to all around him, he is an angel, and has subdued more by his generosity, mildness, and benevolent disposition, than by the force of his arms. He is completely the winner of his own honour and reputation, and assumes to himself the title of Liberator or Salvator, as delivering the country he governs, his own adopted one, from servitude to strangers and tyrants; and his highest ambition is to restore the empire of Bornou to its former splendour and vast extent. His life, however, will most likely be too short for this great work, unless his means for carrying on offensive war should be surprisingly increased. For the last eight years, the sheikh has carried on a very desperate and bloody war with the sultan of Begharmi, who governs a powerful and warlike people, inhabiting a very large tract of country S of Bornou, and on the E bank of the Shary. Although meeting with some reverses, and on one occasion losing his eldest son in these wars, who was greatly beloved by the people, he has, upon the whole, been successful; and is said to have, from first to last, destroyed and led into slavery, more than 30,000 of the sultan of Begharmi's subjects, besides burning his towns and driving off his flocks. The late sultan of B., who always accompanied the sheikh to the field, also lost his life in these wars; his death was attributable to his immense size and weight. The horse he rode refused to move on with him from fatigue, although at the time not more than 500 yards from the gates of Angala, and he fell into the hands of the enemy. He died, however, with great dignity; and six of his eunuchs and as many of his slaves who would not quit him, shared his fate. A sultan of B. carries no arms, and it is beneath his dignity to defend himself: sitting down, therefore, under a tree, with his people around him, he received his enemies, and hiding his face in the shawl which covered his head, was pierced with a hundred spears. Ibrahim, his brother, succeeded him. The sultanship of B., however, is but a name: the court still keeps up considerable state, and adheres strictly to its ancient customs, and this is the only privilege left them. El Kanemy is a most interesting and aspiring chief, and an extraordinary, if not a solitary instance, in the eastern world, of a man raising 3 H himself to sovereign power, from a humble station, without shedding blood by the assassin's knife, or removing those who stood in his way by the bowstring or the poisoned cup." Authorities.] Proceedings of the Africancia Assotion. - Browne's Travels in Africa. - Denham's and Clapperton's Travels.—Travels of R. and J. Lander.
BORNZA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 53 m. ESE of Tchernigov.
BORO, a river of Mongolia, an affluent of the Khara, on the l. bank. It issues from a lake of the same name, and traverses from S to N a plain welladapted to agriculture. It abounds in fish.
BORO, a river of co. Wexford, rising on the E side of the Blackstairs mountains, and flowing into the Slaney after a course of 11 m.

BORO BUDOR, or Boro Bodo, an ancient Buddhist temple, in the island of Java, prov. of Kedu, 18 m. NW of Jokjo-Kerta, or Yugya-Carta. It rises on an eminence in a plain extending from the central mountain ranges to the S coast; and forms a terraced structure of pyramidal form, measuring at the base 526 ft. and 116 ft. in height. It has four entrances corresponding in position to the cardinal points, and guarded by colossal lions. The walls are covered with a profusion of sculptured ornaments; and, in 1815, nearly 400 images of the idol were to be seen within their precincts. With the exception of the dome, the edifice is in nearly a complete state of preservation.
BORODIANKA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 29 m. NW of Kiev, near the l. bank of the Zdvij.

BORODINO, a village of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 70 m. WSW of Moscow, in the dist. and 10 m. W of Mojaisk, on the Kologa, a small affluent of the Moskva. It is celebrated as the scene of a great battle between the Russians and the French on 7th September, 1812. The Russians having collected a force of about 120,000 men, determined to risk a general engagement on this spot, less in the hope of decisive success, than on the calculation of obliging the French to pay dearly for their ground, and weakening them against the approach of winter. On the right of the Russians was the river Moskwa; on their left a thick coppice of underwood, with a battery; the length of their line was about 4 m, protected at intervals by batteries, particularly towards the right. Their position was not particularly strong, the country being flat; but their artillery was ample. Aware that the left was his vulnerable point, Kutusoff, who commanded the Russians, threw up a redoubt about half-a-mile in its front; but the French van got possession of it two days before the battle. Bonaparte reconnoitred the whole position on the 6th, and determined to make his chief attack on the Russian left. On the 7th both armies were drawn up by sun-rise, and the batteries of several hundred field-pieces on each side, opened at 6 o'clock. No sweeping manœuvres took place on this first day. The French, after making an impression with their artillery and musketry, marched forward to attack particular points in the Russian position, not successively, but simultaneously; and the too great length of their line afforded advantage to the enemy accustomed to advance in masses. It was thus the attack on the Russian left was made in front, along the edge of a coppice, instead of taking a circuit, and beginning at the extremity of the flank where Kutusoff expected it, and where he had stationed a protecting corps. The French pushed forward, regardless of loss, and carried several of the batteries; the Russians, reinforced, returned with great intrepidity to attempt their recovery, but in vain. Success fluctuated most in the centre, where the Russians at one time retook a great battery, and drove the French back with dreadful carnage; but the failure of their left enabling the enemy to throw a mass of force into this quarter, the result was an irruption which nothing could withstand, and the final loss of the position. About 8 o'clock in the morning of the second day the French first got possession of the Russian batteries; but the reiterated attacks of the latter prolonged the engagement till noon, and even till 2 o'clock, after which the fighting became partial, and soon ceased altogether on the left and centre. Murat, Ney, and Eugene Beauharnois, distinguished themselves greatly in this battle. On the side of the Russians fell Prince Bagration, a general of great intrepidity and talent. The loss of the Russians was about 30,000: that of the French nearly, if not fully equal. There was no pursuit; the batteries on the Russian right were maintained and continued to sweep a part of the field; but, in the course of the night, the Russians evacuated their position, and took the road to Moscow.
